prs.title: add missing /api/battles route ,fixes Invalid JSON error on battle creation
prs.body:
## Root Cause
The frontend (`dsa-battle-mode.js`) called `/api/battles` for all battle
operations, but no such API route existed in `api/`. The server returned
its default HTML 404 error page. The frontend called `res.json()` on that
HTML response, which threw:

> Unexpected token 'T', "The page c..." is not valid JSON

`battleService.js` existed in `pages/Dsa Battle/` but was never wired
to any HTTP route.

## Fix
Created `api/battles.js` as a Vercel serverless function that:
- Handles all 5 routes the frontend already calls
- Uses identical auth pattern to `api/session.js` (cookie + HMAC JWT)
- Uses identical Firebase init pattern to other `api/` files
- Replicates the transaction logic from `battleService.js` directly

## Routes added
| Method | Path | Action |
|--------|------|--------|
| POST | `/api/battles` | Create battle |
| GET | `/api/battles/history` | Battle history |
| GET | `/api/battles/:id` | Poll battle state |
| POST | `/api/battles/:id/join` | Join battle |
| POST | `/api/battles/:id/submit` | Submit solution |

## Testing
1. Go to DSA Battle Mode → Battle Lobby
2. Select difficulty, enter opponent email, click Create Battle
3. Battle ID returned, no JSON parse error
4. Opponent can join via battle ID
5. Both players can submit solution

## Related issue
Fixes #905

* **New Features**
  * Added a new battle feature with endpoints to create, join, view, submit, and review battle history.

  * Requests to battle-related API paths are now routed correctly for cleaner access.

* **Bug Fixes**

prs.title: build false positive classifier training script
prs.body: > **Before opening:** make sure there is an issue tracking this work, and link it below. PRs without a linked issue may be closed without review. ## Linked issue Closes #163 ## What this PR does This PR introduces the automated training pipeline for the False Positive classifier. It securely extracts human-labeled findings from the local SQLite database, generates contextual deep-learning embeddings using `microsoft/codebert-base`, and trains a Logistic Regression model. The trained model is then serialized and exported as `fp_classifier.pkl` to be used for live inference. ## Type of change - [ ] Bug fix - [ ] New feature - [x] ML model / training pipeline - [ ] Refactor (no behaviour change) - [ ] Documentation - [ ] Tests only ## ML tier (if applicable) - [x] Tier 1 — Triage - [ ] Tier 2 — Predictive - [ ] Tier 3 — Autonomous - [ ] Not ML-related ## Stack affected - [x] Backend - [ ] Frontend - [ ] Both --- ## Changes ### Backend - Added `scripts/train_fp_classifier.py` to handle data extraction, embedding generation, and model training. - Implemented dynamic hardware acceleration detection (MPS/CUDA/CPU) for optimal `transformers` execution. - Added data-sanitization logic to gracefully handle SQLite boolean/string type casting quirks. - Added a configurable `--batch-size` parameter to prevent Out-Of-Memory (OOM) errors during CodeBERT embedding generation. - Implemented mathematical safeguards to abort gracefully if the dataset lacks sufficient stratification (requires a minimum of 2 examples per class). ### Frontend - N/A ### New dependencies - `transformers`: Required to load and execute the `microsoft/codebert-base` tokenizer and model. - `torch`: Required as the computation backend for Hugging Face tensors. - `tqdm`: Added to provide user-friendly CLI progress bars during the lengthy embedding generation phase. ### Database / schema changes - N/A --- ## Testing **How did you test this?** Tested locally by executing `python scripts/train_fp_classifier.py --min-samples 2`. - Verified the script's edge-case safeguard successfully aborted execution when only one label class existed. - Verified Hugging Face assets (`pytorch_model.bin`, etc.) successfully downloaded and cached to the local environment. - Verified CodeBERT embeddings generated successfully without memory leaks. - Verified `fp_classifier.pkl` was saved precisely into the `backend/app/ml/` directory. - Verified the root `.gitignore` update successfully prevents the massive `.pkl` binary from being tracked by git. **Checklist** - [ ] Tested locally end-to-end (upload ZIP or GitHub URL → scan → findings returned correctly) - [ ] New ML model falls back gracefully when model file is absent - [x] No new `console.error` or unhandled Python exceptions introduced - [ ] Added or updated tests where applicable - [x] `requirements.txt` / `package.json` updated if new dependencies added - [x] New model files (`.pkl`, `.pt`, etc.) are gitignored, not committed --- ## Anything reviewers should focus on Please review the SQLite data coercion logic (`df["false_positive"].map(...)`). Because SQLite doesn't enforce strict native boolean types, I explicitly mapped string representations (`"true"`, `"false"`) and Python types (`True`, `1`) to standard integers to ensure `scikit-learn` operates safely. Also, note that the CodeBERT `batch_size` defaults to 16, which is generally safe, but users on lower-VRAM hardware can easily lower it via the CLI. ## Screenshots (if UI changed)

prs.title: evaluate tool response errors to prevent silent failures
prs.body: **The Problem:** Agent execution handlers were previously blinding the workflow engine. External dependencies (like MCP servers, LLMs, or browser tools) that failed gracefully by returning structured error objects were being swallowed because the handlers hardcoded `success: true` at the end of their execution blocks. This caused the state machine to incorrectly pass broken data to downstream nodes, triggering unpredictable cascading failures. **The Solution:** This PR rewires the handler resolution logic across the execution layer to dynamically evaluate the response payload. If an error is detected from the underlying tool or API, the step is correctly flagged as `success: false` and the exact error is surfaced, allowing the workflow to safely halt or route accordingly. **Key Changes:** * **`llm.handler.js` & `document.handler.js`:** Now inspect `llmRes.error` before resolving. Added strict evaluation to safely handle empty string `""` generations (preventing falsy fallbacks). Ensured failed generations are no longer accidentally saved to the agent's long-term memory. * **`mcp.handler.js`, `browser.handler.js`, & `tool.handler.js`:** Added safe navigation (`?.error`) to detect structured tool failures (e.g., Puppeteer timeouts, missing MCP binaries) and correctly pipe the error strings to the router. * **`email.handler.js`:** Added active inspection of the NodeMailer `info.rejected` array to catch and flag partial SMTP rejections that do not throw hard Node.js exceptions. **Validation:** - Tested locally by forcing an SMTP `ECONNREFUSED` error in an email node connected to downstream LLM nodes. - Verified that the engine correctly marks the failing step in the DB/UI and instantly halts the pipeline execution, preventing downstream nodes from firing blindly. Closes #191

prs.title: preserve successful step state on retry to prevent duplication
prs.body: ## Fix: Preserve successful steps on retry to prevent duplication **The Problem:** Previously, when a multi-step task failed, `queueService` completely wiped the `stepResults` array. This caused the runner to lose its state during retries, leading it to re-execute previously successful (and potentially non-idempotent) steps, such as sending emails or making duplicate API calls. **The Solution:** This PR implements deterministic state preservation so the runner can safely fast-forward through already-completed steps during a retry, while still accurately failing and re-trying the broken steps. **Changes made:** * **`backend/src/agents/queueService.js`**: Instead of emptying `stepResults` on failure, it now filters the archived steps and retains only those where `success === true`. *(Note: the complete sequence, including the failure, is still safely archived in the `retryHistory` array for audit logging).* * **`backend/src/agents/runner.js`**: Hardened the `cachedResult` lookup logic to explicitly require `r.success === true` before fast-forwarding, ensuring that failed steps are never accidentally skipped. Closes #186 testing ->

prs.title: resolve silent file overwrites by implementing strict action switch
prs.body:
## Description
This PR addresses a critical vulnerability in the file handler agent where unsupported actions (such as `remove` or `list` dispatched from the frontend) were silently falling through an `else` block and executing `fs.writeFileSync`. This resulted in unintended file contents being overwritten or destroyed. 

The control flow has been refactored to use a strict `switch` statement to explicitly handle supported operations and safely reject unknown commands.

## Changes Made
* **Refactored Control Flow:** Replaced the unsafe `if/else` catch-all in `backend/src/agents/handlers/file.handler.js` with a strict `switch(config.action)`.
* **Added `remove` Support:** Implemented native `fs.rmSync(filePath, { force: true })` to safely handle file deletion requests.
* **Added `list` Support:** Implemented `fs.readdirSync` to return directory contents when requested.
* **Graceful Error Handling:** Added a `default` case that intercepts unsupported actions and safely returns a `success: false` step result instead of executing a destructive filesystem operation.

## Related Issue
Closes #185

prs.title: feat(auth): implement identity-based rate limiting for auth endpoints
prs.body: ## Summary This PR implements strict, identity-aware rate limiting for authentication and password-reset endpoints. Standard DRF IP-based throttling is insufficient against distributed attacks (like proxy-hopping). This update introduces custom throttle classes that rate-limit based on the target identity (email/username) regardless of the incoming IP address, effectively neutralizing credential stuffing and targeted email-bombing attacks. ## Related Issue Closes #456 ## Changes Made * **`backend/apps/accounts/throttles.py`**: Added `StrictIdentityLoginThrottle` and `StrictIdentityPasswordResetThrottle` which extract the `email` or `username` from the request payload to use as the cache key. * **`backend/apps/accounts/views.py`**: Injected the new strict throttles into `LoginView` and `PasswordResetRequestView`, creating a dual-layer defense (IP-based + Identity-based). ## Testing 1. Run the local backend development server. 2. Open a terminal and run a bash loop to simulate a brute-force attack against a single email: ````bash for i in {1..7}; do curl -s -X POST [http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/auth/login/](http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/auth/login/) -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"email":"lakshaytest@gmail.com","password":"wrong"}'; done ```` 3. Verify that the first 5 attempts return a `401 Unauthorized` and subsequent attempts immediately return a `429 Too Many Requests` (Expected available in X seconds). - [x] Tested manually - [ ] Add new unit/integration test - [x] verified existing test still pass ## Screenshots *Backend security update only. No visual UI changes.* ## Checklist - [ ] Tests added or updated - [ ] Documentation updated if needed - [x] No secrets committed

prs.title: Trace IDs and Structured Execution Logging (phase 4.4)
prs.body: ### Description This PR implements **Phase 4.4: Trace IDs and Structured Execution Logging**, the final deliverable for the Phase 4 observability roadmap (#159). It introduces unique, end-to-end trace identifiers to make debugging concurrent, multi-step workflows painless. ### Implementation Details * **Native Generation:** Utilized Node's built-in `crypto.randomUUID()` at the workflow entry point (`runner.js`) to generate the `traceId` without introducing new dependencies. * **Context Propagation:** Injected the trace ID directly into the shared execution `context` object, ensuring it flows naturally through the dispatcher and all handlers. * **Centralized Logging (No `console.log` sprawl):** Per the maintainer's request, I avoided scattering trace-aware `console.log` statements across the codebase. Instead, I upgraded the existing shared `logger.js` utility. The logger now extracts the `traceId` from the metadata and formats the terminal output automatically (e.g., `ℹ️ [Trace: ] ...`). * **No Database Bloat:** The trace ID is strictly maintained as an ephemeral execution-context and console debugging tool. It is intentionally *not* persisted to the MongoDB schema. Fixes #180

prs.title: implement step-level telemetry and metrics (Phase 4.3)
prs.body: ### Description This PR implements **Phase 4.3 (Step-Level Execution Metrics)**, introducing centralized telemetry to track execution performance across the pipeline. ### Implementation Details * Utilized Node's native `perf_hooks` for high-resolution, sub-millisecond timing. * The timer is initialized centrally within the dispatcher (`executor.js`) immediately before the execution loop, ensuring consistent metrics across all handler types without code duplication. ### Addressing Edge Cases (Retries, Timeouts, Failures) `durationMs` was implemented to accurately track the **total wall-clock time** a workflow spends stalled on a step. * **Successes/Approvals:** Captures the exact execution lifespan. * **Retries & Timeouts:** The timer specifically wraps the entire retry loop boundary. If a step fails and retries, `durationMs` accurately records the total time spent attempting the step (including all execution attempts + backoff sleep delays) before the final failure boundary is reached. Fixes #167

prs.title: implement standardized error classes and handler boundaries
prs.body: ### Description This PR implements **Phase 4.2 (Standardized Error Classes & Boundaries)** for the execution engine. It wraps the step execution logic in a robust try/catch boundary to normalize failures into structured data, laying the groundwork for programmatic retries and telemetry in future phases. Per the maintainer's architectural requirements, this implementation is **100% backward-compatible**. It intentionally retains the legacy string-based error formats to ensure the existing UI and execution history timelines remain completely unaffected. ### Changes Made * **`backend/src/agents/utils/errors.js`**: Created a base `ExecutionError` class and specific subclasses (`TimeoutError`, `ValidationError`, `NetworkError`) to standardize error structures. * **`backend/src/agents/executor.js`**: Wrapped the handler execution logic in a standardized error boundary. Preserved legacy `output` and `error` strings while appending a new structured `errorMetadata` object to the step execution result. * **`backend/src/models/task.model.js`**: Updated the Mongoose `StepResultSchema` to explicitly define the `error` and `errorMetadata` fields so they successfully save to the database without being stripped by strict mode. ### Testing * Forced a step failure locally to verify the error boundary successfully catches and normalizes the error. * Verified in the frontend UI that the execution timeline renders the historical string outputs perfectly without crashing. * Inspected the API response payload to confirm the new `errorMetadata` JSON object is successfully being saved to MongoDB and returned alongside the legacy fields. Fixes #161

prs.title: restrict Helmet CSP imgSrc to explicit Cloudinary domain

labels.name: backend
labels.description: For changes related to the Express server, API routes, or MongoDB models

labels.name: security
labels.description: For issues related to vulnerabilities (like updating an out-of-date npm package)

prs.body:
Fixes #174

### What happened
The original bug (Cloudinary product images broken in production due to Helmet's default CSP only allowing `'self'`) is not currently reproducible — PR #235 (unrelated OAuth/login fix, merged 2026-06-15) added a CSP config that includes a generic `"https:"` wildcard in `imgSrc`, which incidentally already permits Cloudinary's origin.

However, that wildcard allows **any** https source, not an explicit Cloudinary whitelist as requested in this issue's Notes section - defeating the purpose of a tightened CSP.

### Fix
`BACKEND/app.js` — `imgSrc` directive in the Helmet `contentSecurityPolicy` config:

```diff
- imgSrc: ["'self'", "data:", "blob:", "https:"],
+ imgSrc: ["'self'", "data:", "blob:", "https://res.cloudinary.com", "https://via.placeholder.com"],
```

- `https://res.cloudinary.com` — explicit whitelist per the issue's request.
- `https://via.placeholder.com` — also added, since it's used as `fallbackSrc` in `RecentlyViewedCarousel.jsx`, `RelatedProducts.jsx`, `ProductPage.jsx`, and `MyOrdersPage.jsx`. Narrowing `imgSrc` without it would've broken those fallback images, a regression the issue doesn't ask for.

### Testing
- Ran app in production mode, confirmed Cloudinary product images render with no CSP console errors.
- Confirmed placeholder fallback images still load when a product image fails.
- No other directives (`scriptSrc`, `connectSrc`, `crossOriginEmbedderPolicy`, etc.) were touched.

prs.title: Feat/parallel workflow execution
prs.body: closes #129 ## Description This PR introduces full support for concurrent workflow execution, allowing the engine to process independent branches in parallel and merge them back together. It includes updates to both the Next.js visual builder and the backend execution engine. ## What was changed ### Frontend (UI & Builder) * **Added New Node Types:** Introduced `Parallel` and `Join` nodes to the Visual Builder. * **Payload Normalization:** Updated the `saveWorkflow` mapping in `page.tsx` to ensure `Parallel` and `Join` nodes are correctly formatted and sent to the API instead of falling back to `'unknown'`. * **UI Polish:** Updated the workflow step rendering logic so the canvas correctly displays the labels for the new nodes. * **Graph Validation:** Modified `graphValidation.ts` to properly validate "diamond" topologies (splitting and merging paths). ### Backend (Execution Engine) * **Concurrent Processing:** Upgraded `executor.js` to detect `parallel` nodes and dispatch multiple branch executions simultaneously, waiting for all to complete before proceeding to the `join` node. * **Type Normalization:** Patched `runner.js` to defensively convert all step types to lowercase upon task claim, preventing case-sensitivity execution failures. ## How to test 1. Run the frontend (`npm run dev`) and the backend worker (`node src/agents/runner.js`). 2. Open the Visual Builder and construct a "diamond" graph: `Parallel -> [Delay 1, Delay 2] -> Join` 3. Save the workflow. 4. Execute the workflow and monitor the worker logs. You will see both delay branches executing concurrently, followed by a successful join and task completion.

prs.body:
> **Before opening:** make sure there is an issue tracking this work, and link it below. PRs without a linked issue may be closed without review.

## Linked issue

Closes #95 

## What this PR does

This PR replaces the heavy 3-second database short-polling mechanism for organization batch scans with real-time Server-Sent Events (SSE). It also introduces a resilient "circuit breaker" abort feature, allowing users to gracefully cancel massive organization scans mid-flight without leaving zombie processes, freezing the UI, or exhausting SQLite thread pools.

## Type of change

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] New feature
- [ ] ML model / training pipeline
- [ ] Refactor (no behaviour change)
- [ ] Documentation

## ML tier (if applicable)

- [ ] Tier 2 — Predictive

- [x] Not ML-related

## Stack affected

- [ ] Backend
- [ ] Frontend
- [x] Both

---

## Changes

### Backend

- Added `GET /api/scans/org/{org_job_id}/stream` endpoint utilizing FastAPI's `StreamingResponse` for SSE.
- Added `POST /api/scans/org/{org_job_id}/abort` endpoint with a 5-attempt retry loop to handle SQLite `database is locked` exceptions under high concurrency.
- Updated `_run_repo_scan_task` and `_run_org_batch` to implement a Graceful Shutdown (instantly killing `pending` jobs while allowing actively `scanning` jobs to resolve and save their data).
- Ensured strict per-tick database connection closures inside the SSE generator loop to prevent thread exhaustion.

### Frontend

- Removed `setInterval` polling logic from `dashboard.tsx` and implemented native `EventSource` API to consume the SSE stream.
- Added strict `useEffect` cleanup to automatically close the `EventSource` on component unmount to prevent memory leaks.
- Added a destructive "Cancel Scan" button to the tracking modal with responsive hover, scale, and active UI states.
- Updated the modal UI to correctly reflect a red `ABORTED` state while intelligently keeping the stream alive until the final active background workers finish.

### New dependencies

- 

### Database / schema changes

- 

---

## Testing

**How did you test this?**

Tested locally end-to-end by initiating a massive organization scan (e.g., Netflix / Microsoft) and triggering the new "Cancel Scan" abort mechanism while the backend spinners were active. Observed that queued (`pending`) jobs instantly snapped to aborted, active (`scanning`) jobs finished gracefully, and the `EventSource` connection closed cleanly once the cluster resolved. Tested database lock resilience by forcing concurrent writes during the abort sequence. Added unit tests for both new endpoints.

**Checklist**

- [x] Tested locally end-to-end (upload ZIP or GitHub URL → scan → findings returned correctly)
- [ ] New ML model falls back gracefully when model file is absent
- [x] No new `console.error` or unhandled Python exceptions introduced

## Anything reviewers should focus on

Pay close attention to the `stream_org_status` async generator in `backend/app/main.py`. Notice that `db = await get_db()` and `await db.close()` are intentionally executed *inside* the `while True` loop on every single tick. This design decision was explicitly made to prevent `aiosqlite` from throwing a `RuntimeError: threads can only be started once` exception under long-running stream loads.

prs.title: feat: implement entropy-based secret scanning engin
prs.body: > **Before opening:** make sure there is an issue tracking this work, and link it below. PRs without a linked issue may be closed without review. ## Linked issue Closes #21 ## What this PR does Implemented a fallback entropy-based secret scanner to catch highly random strings (like custom enterprise API keys) that don't match standard regex prefixes. It calculates Shannon Entropy on hardcoded strings across the repository and flags those exceeding the mathematical threshold, while aggressively filtering out high-entropy noise (like UUIDs, SVGs, and hex colors) to prevent false positives. ## Type of change - [ ] Bug fix - [x] New feature - [ ] ML model / training pipeline - [ ] Refactor (no behaviour change) - [ ] Documentation - [ ] Tests only ## ML tier (if applicable) - [ ] Tier 1 — Triage - [ ] Tier 2 — Predictive - [ ] Tier 3 — Autonomous - [x] Not ML-related --- ## Changes ### Backend - Added `backend/app/scanners/entropy.py` containing the Shannon Entropy mathematical engine and the false-positive allowlist logic. - Injected `run_entropy` into the main scanning pipeline in `backend/app/main.py` (`_scan_repo_dir`, `/scan`, `/scan-url`, `/verify`). - Mapped new entropy findings to seamlessly integrate with the existing `Finding` Pydantic schema (setting `category="secret"` and tracking the raw entropy score in `metadata`). --- ## Testing **How did you test this?** 1. Built a local Python unit test suite (`test_entropy.py`) to verify the Shannon Entropy math and ensure the allowlist properly bypasses standard UUIDs, Hex codes, and SVG paths while successfully catching the target 22-character secret from Issue #21. 2. Ran a full E2E test locally by spinning up the FastAPI backend and Vite frontend, uploading a poisoned `.zip` repository containing a hardcoded secret, and verified the finding populated perfectly in the UI dashboard with a `HIGH` severity. **Checklist** - [x] Tested locally end-to-end (upload ZIP or GitHub URL → scan → findings returned correctly) - [x] No new `console.error` or unhandled Python exceptions introduced --- ## Anything reviewers should focus on **Note on the threshold limit:** I adjusted the trigger threshold from Srijan's initially proposed `> 4.5` down to `> 4.0`. The maximum theoretical Shannon Entropy for a 22-character string (like the target secret `z8Fk92nV_p0xLM391_v8wZ`) is log2(22) = 4.45. It is mathematically impossible for a string of that length to hit 4.5. The robust allowlist ensures this slightly lower threshold does not introduce false positive spam.

prs.title: feat(backend): implement reachability analysis for vulnerable dependencies
prs.body: > **Before opening:** make sure there is an issue tracking this work, and link it below. PRs without a linked issue may be closed without review. ## Linked issue Closes #22 ## What this PR does This PR introduces a reachability analysis engine that triggers after the `osv-scanner` finishes. It actively parses the source code directory to detect if flagged vulnerable packages are actually being imported (e.g., `require('pkg')` or `import 'pkg'`), helping developers distinguish between passive, unused dependencies and actively executing vulnerabilities. ## Type of change - [ ] Bug fix - [x] New feature - [ ] ML model / training pipeline - [ ] Refactor (no behaviour change) - [ ] Documentation - [ ] Tests only ## ML tier (if applicable) - [ ] Tier 1 — Triage - [ ] Tier 2 — Predictive - [ ] Tier 3 — Autonomous - [x] Not ML-related --- ## Changes ### Backend - **`app/models.py`:** Extended the `Finding` schema with a new `Reachability` Pydantic model (`reachable` boolean and `evidence` string). - **`app/utils/fs.py`:** Added `check_reachability`, a highly optimized regex parser. It uses `os.walk` with in-place pruning of heavy directories (`node_modules`, `.git`, `venv`, etc.) to quickly find active imports in target source files without tanking performance. - **`app/scanners/osv.py`:** Implemented a post-scan hook that iterates over the OSV findings, runs the reachability check for each vulnerable package, and mutates the finding object before returning it. --- ## Testing **How did you test this?** - Created a local, isolated dummy Node.js project. - Installed an outdated version of `lodash` (`4.17.20`) with known vulnerabilities. - Created an `index.js` file containing `const _ = require('lodash');`. - Executed the `run_osv_scanner` directly against the dummy project and verified the final JSON payload successfully appended: `"reachability": { "reachable": true, "evidence": "Imported in index.js: line 1" }`. see the screenshot - **Checklist** - [x] Tested locally end-to-end (upload ZIP or GitHub URL → scan → findings returned correctly) - [ ] New ML model falls back gracefully when model file is absent - [x] No new `console.error` or unhandled Python exceptions introduced - [ ] Added or updated tests where applicable - [ ] `requirements.txt` / `package.json` updated if new dependencies added - [ ] New model files (`.pkl`, `.pt`, etc.) are gitignored, not committed ---

prs.title: fix(backend): restrict CORS policy to trusted frontend origins

labels.name: backend
labels.description: For changes related to the Express server, API routes, or MongoDB models

prs.body: Closes #61 ### What Changed This PR addresses a critical security vulnerability by restricting the backend CORS policy. * **Removed Wildcard:** Replaced the default `app.use(cors())` wildcard configuration in `BACKEND/app.js` with a dynamic whitelist strategy. * **Dynamic Origins:** Configured an `allowedOrigins` array that explicitly trusts the local Vite development server (`http://localhost:5173`) and the production domain via `process.env.FRONTEND_URL`. * **Same-Origin Fallback:** Included an `if (!origin)` check to ensure same-origin requests (like those from the statically served frontend in production) are not falsely flagged and blocked. ### Why It Changed As outlined in Issue #61, leaving CORS completely open exposes the API to unauthorized cross-origin requests from any random domain. By explicitly restricting origins to trusted environments, the API is now secured against malicious external client-side requests in production, while maintaining a seamless developer experience locally. ### Testing & Verification * **Local Developer Experience:** Verified that the Vite frontend (`localhost:5173`) connects to the API seamlessly. * **Security Audit:** Simulated a cross-origin attack using `curl -H "Origin: http://evil-hacker.com" http://localhost:5001/api/products` and verified the server correctly intercepts and rejects the request with a `CORS Policy Error: Origin not allowed` exception. Error came as expected.

prs.body: Closes #30 ### What Changed This PR completely migrates the shopping cart state management from React Context to Zustand. * **New Store:** Created a standalone Zustand store (`src/store/cart.js`) to handle all cart logic and state. * **Local Storage Upgrade:** Replaced the manual `useEffect` and `JSON.parse` logic with Zustand's built-in `persist` middleware for safer, automated local storage syncing. * **Provider Removal:** Stripped the obsolete `` wrapper from `src/main.jsx` and deleted the `src/context/CartContext.jsx` file. * **Zero Regressions:** Exported a custom `useCart` bridge hook from the new store that perfectly mimics the old Context's return signature. This allowed us to swap the imports across UI components (e.g., `Navbar.jsx`, `ProductCard.jsx`) without rewriting any component logic. ### Why It Changed As outlined in Issue #30, relying on React Context at the root of the application for cart state was causing unnecessary re-renders across the entire component tree. By migrating to Zustand, the cart state now lives entirely outside the React tree. Components only re-render when the specific state they subscribe to changes, resulting in a cleaner, more scalable, and highly performant frontend architecture. ### Testing & Verification * **Production Ready:** Confirmed the production build compiles flawlessly with zero unresolved import errors (`npm run build`). * **UI Integrity:** Verified that adding and removing items updates the Navbar cart count instantly, exactly as it did before. * **Persistence:** Verified that the Zustand `persist` middleware correctly hydrates cart data from `localStorage` by closing the browser, reloading the app, and ensuring the cart remained intact. build test successfully passed.

prs.title: perf: optimize UI re-renders and global state management
prs.body:
### Fixes #20: Optimize Workflow Dashboard Performance & Reduce Unnecessary Re-renders

**Optimizations Checklist**
- [x] **1. Reduce Unnecessary Re-renders:** Implemented `useMemo` and `useCallback` across key components.
- [x] **2. Optimize Workflow List Rendering:** Memoized dashboard lists and workflow mappings to prevent UI churn. *(Note: Debounced search skipped as no search UI currently exists).*
- [x] **3. Improve Builder Performance:** Stabilized React Flow handlers for smoother node dragging.
- [x] **4. State Management Optimization:** Memoized `AssistantContext` and `SettingsContext` to prevent global cascading renders.
- [x] **5. Performance Monitoring:** Engineered and implemented `usePerformanceMonitor` hook for render count and timing logs.

---

**1. State Management Optimization**
*   **`frontend/src/context/assistant-context.tsx`**: Applied `useMemo` to the context provider value and `useCallback` to helper functions to prevent global cascading re-renders across consumers.
*   **`frontend/src/context/SettingsContext.tsx`**: Memoized the context provider value and `load` function to stabilize global references.

**2. Optimize List & Dashboard Rendering**
*   **`frontend/src/app/workflows/page.tsx`**: Wrapped the `renderedWorkflows` mapping in `useMemo`. Opening modals, clicking dropdowns, or copying IDs no longer forces a re-render of the entire workflow list.
*   **`frontend/src/app/dashboard/page.tsx`**: Memoized derived arrays and wrapped UI helper functions in `useCallback` to prevent unnecessary component churn.

**3. Performance Monitoring & Builder**
*   **`frontend/src/hooks/usePerformanceMonitor.ts`**: Created a custom hook to output render counts and exact millisecond timing logs.
*   **`frontend/src/components/workflow/visual-builder.tsx`**: Injected the `usePerformanceMonitor` hook to profile rendering performance and track visual builder overhead.

prs.title: fix: allow application/pdf in uploadMiddleware for resume uploads
prs.body:
Fixes #36 

### Problem
The `/analyze` endpoint was rejecting PDF files because `uploadMiddleware.js` only allowed image MIME types (`image/jpeg`, `image/png`, `image/jpg`). This prevented valid PDF resumes from reaching the controller.

### Changes
- **`uploadMiddleware.js`** — Added a separate `uploadResume` multer instance with `memoryStorage` and `application/pdf` file filter. Existing `upload` instance for images is untouched.
- **`resumeRoutes.js`** — Updated `/analyze` route to use `uploadResume.single("resume")` instead of `upload.single("resume")`.

## Why memoryStorage
Since the controller reads req.file.buffer to send the PDF to Gemini, diskStorage won't work here -it saves to disk and never fills buffer, meaning the file would silently fail even after the MIME fix.

### Testing
- Upload a `.pdf` via `/api/resume/analyze` → accepted and analyzed 
- Upload a non-PDF via `/api/resume/analyze` → rejected with error 
- Other routes using `upload` (image uploads) → unaffected

prs.title: fix: validate GitHub URL and check repo reachability in /scan-url
prs.body:
> **Before opening:** make sure there is an issue tracking this work, and link it below. PRs without a linked issue may be closed without review.

## Linked issue
Closes #5 

## What this PR does
<!-- 2–4 sentences. What changed and why? Focus on the "what" — the issue already explains the "why". -->
The `/scan-url` endpoint was silently accepting invalid or private GitHub URLs, causing the server to hang or return a generic 500 with no useful message. This PR adds URL format validation, a reachability check before cloning, and a 30 second timeout on the download step. The frontend now also parses the error response correctly so users see a readable message instead of raw JSON.

## Type of change
- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] New feature
- [ ] ML model / training pipeline
- [ ] Refactor (no behaviour change)
- [ ] Documentation
- [ ] Tests only

## ML tier (if applicable)
- [ ] Tier 1 — Triage
- [ ] Tier 2 — Predictive
- [ ] Tier 3 — Autonomous
- [x] Not ML-related

---

## Changes

### Backend
<!-- List the meaningful backend changes. Delete if none. -->
- Added regex validation on the URL format in `github_zip_url` — returns 422 immediately if the format is invalid
- Added `check_repo_reachable()` — sends a HEAD request to `https://github.com/{owner}/{repo}` with a 5 second timeout before attempting to download, returns 422 if repo is not found or private
- Wrapped `download_to_path` in `asyncio.wait_for` with a 30 second timeout — returns 504 "Repository clone timed out" if exceeded.

### Frontend
<!-- List the meaningful frontend changes. Delete if none. -->
- `scanRepoUrl` in `api.ts` now parses error responses as JSON and surfaces the `detail` field so the actual error message is shown to the user instead of a raw JSON string.

---

## Testing

**How did you test this?**
<!-- Describe what you ran, on what kind of repo/ZIP, and what you observed. Screenshots welcome. -->
- Submitted a completely invalid URL (e.g. `not-a-url`) → got 422 immediately with clear message
- Submitted a nonexistent repo (e.g. `https://github.com/someone/this-does-not-exist`) → got 422 "Repository not found or is private" within 5 seconds
- Submitted a valid public repo → scan proceeded normally.

**Checklist**
- [x] Tested locally end-to-end (upload ZIP or GitHub URL → scan → findings returned correctly)
- [ ] New ML model falls back gracefully when model file is absent
- [x] No new `console.error` or unhandled Python exceptions introduced
- [ ] Added or updated tests where applicable
- [x] `requirements.txt` / `package.json` updated if new dependencies added
- [ ] New model files (`.pkl`, `.pt`, etc.) are gitignored, not committed

---

## Anything reviewers should focus on
<!-- Is there a design decision you're unsure about? A tricky piece of logic? Tell reviewers where to look. -->
The `check_repo_reachable` function uses a HEAD request which works for public repos. Private repos return 404 from GitHub's public API which is the correct behaviour — we surface that as "Repository not found or is private" to avoid leaking information.

prs.title: feat: implement recursive bst_delete and integrate into binary_search_tree_Demo
prs.body:
## Summary
Closes #20

## Changes
- Added recursive `bst_delete` function in `bst.c` handling all three deletion cases
- Declared `bst_delete` in `bst.h`
- Integrated deletion into `binary_search_tree_Demo` as option `4` with proper input validation and inorder traversal printed after deletion

## Approach
Followed the recursive approach as specified. Three cases handled:
- **Case 1 (leaf node):** free the node, return NULL
- **Case 2 (single child):** replace node with its only child
- **Case 3 (both children):** find inorder successor (smallest node in right subtree), copy its value, recursively delete it from right subtree

prs.body:

prs.title: feat: add native GitHub, Slack & Discord workflow integration steps
prs.body:
closes #11 

## What changed

Added native integration support for GitHub, Slack, and Discord as first-class workflow step types.
(added single lined comments also to separate all three)
**Backend:**
- Created `backend/src/integrations/github.js` — handles `create_issue`, `get_issue`, `comment_issue` using GitHub REST API
- Created `backend/src/integrations/slack.js` — handles `send_message` via Slack webhook
- Created `backend/src/integrations/discord.js` — handles `send_message` via Discord webhook
- Wired all three into `backend/src/agents/executor.js` following the existing `if (step.type === "...")` pattern
- Added `GITHUB_TOKEN`, `SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL`, `DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL` to `.env.example`

**Frontend:**
- Added GitHub, Slack, Discord as selectable step types in the workflow builder
- Configured action-specific fields for each integration
- Integrated into `saveWorkflow()` and `fetchWorkflow()` so steps persist and reload correctly

## Why it matters

This moves the platform from a pure AI workflow engine toward a real-world automation platform by letting workflows interact with actual developer tools — closing the loop between AI reasoning and external actions.

## Trade-offs

- Kept adapters isolated in `src/integrations/` — no logic leaks into the executor beyond the `if` block
- No OAuth, no SDKs, no abstractions — plain axios calls with env-based credentials as specified
- Notion is out of scope for this PR, can be added separately
- Scoped strictly to basic-level as described in the issue

Closes #66922 
<!-- Feel free to add any additional description of changes below this line -->
## Description

The suggested solution for the "Create a Doubly Linked List" challenge was not passing the tests due to issues in the `remove` method.

## Issue
- The `prev` pointer of the next node was not updated when removing a node
- Unsafe operations could cause errors when `next` or `prev` was `null`
- Edge cases like removing the only node did not correctly reset both `head` and `tail`

## Fix

- Added missing pointer update:
   `if (tempNode.next) {tempNode.next.prev = prevNode;}`
- Added null-safe handling for head removal:
   `if (this.head) {this.head.prev = null; } else {this.tail = null;}`
- Added null-safe handling for tail removal:
   `if (this.tail) {this.tail.next = null; } else { this.head = null;}`

Closes #66899 

<!-- Feel free to add any additional description of changes below this line -->

### Description

One of the tests for the Lightbox Viewer challenge dispatches a click event without enabling bubbling:

```js
galleryItem.dispatchEvent(new Event("click"));
```

This causes valid solutions that use event delegation on the .gallery element to fail, since the event does not bubble up to the parent.

prs.title: Fix: CS8618 guidance for events by adding event-specific diagnostic without 'required' suggestion

labels.name: Community
labels.description: The pull request was submitted by a contributor who is not a Microsoft employee.

prs.body:
## Summary

- This PR fixes incorrect guidance in the CS8618 warning when applied to **events**.

When a non-nullable event is not initialized in the constructor, the compiler
currently reports CS8618 and suggests adding the `required` modifier.

However, events cannot be marked `required`, and doing so produces:
```
CS0106: The modifier 'required' is not valid for this item
```

This results in misleading guidance for users.

This change introduces a new diagnostic that is used specifically for events,
with corrected wording that does **not** suggest the `required` modifier.


---

## Behavior Before vs After

| Member kind       | Diagnostic | Guidance |
|------------------|---------|---------|
| Field / Property | CS8618 (`WRN_UninitializedNonNullableField`) | may suggest `required` |
| Event            | **New diagnostic** (`ERR_NonNullableEventNotInitialized`) | suggests initializing or making nullable — no `required` |

The new event-specific message now states:

> "Consider declaring the event as nullable or initializing it."


---

## Implementation Details

✔️ Added new diagnostic ID in `ErrorCode.cs`  
✔️ Added corresponding resource entry in `CSharpResources.resx`  
✔️ Updated `NullableWalker` to:

- continue emitting CS8618 for fields + properties
- emit the new diagnostic for `SymbolKind.Event`

No existing diagnostic IDs were renumbered or modified.

Nullable analysis behavior is unchanged — only message guidance differs.


---

## Motivation

Events cannot use the `required` modifier, so referencing it in CS8618
produces invalid advice and a follow-up compiler error.

This aligns diagnostic guidance with C# language rules and improves UX,
similar to prior nullable-diagnostic refinements.


---

## Compatibility

- Existing CS8618 behavior maintained
- Existing suppressions and warning codes unaffected
- The new diagnostic applies **only to events**
- The change is scoped and backwards-compatible


---

## Fixes

✔️ fixes #81679

prs.title: Fix: Nullability_PropertyAccess_07 test to expect no warnings

labels.name: Community
labels.description: The pull request was submitted by a contributor who is not a Microsoft employee.

prs.body:
## Issue:
- When using extension types with a nullable receiver (e.g., object?), the compiler was incorrectly inferring the type parameter as non-nullable. This resulted in false positive CS8625: Cannot convert null literal to non-nullable reference type warnings when assigning null to properties defined within the extension type, as the property type was treated as non-nullable.
## Fix:
- This PR ensures that type inference for extension types correctly propagates nullability from the receiver to the type arguments. Specifically, when the receiver is a nullable reference type, the inferred type argument is now also nullable. This allows properties of that type to correctly accept null values without generating warnings.

## Changes:
- Added regression test Nullability_PropertyAccess_07 to src/Compilers/CSharp/Test/Emit/Emit/CompilationEmitTests.cs.
- The test asserts that the following scenario compiles with no diagnostics:
```
#nullable enable
object? oNotNull = new object();
oNotNull.P = null; // Should not warn

static class E
{
    extension<T>(T t)
    {
        public T P { set => throw null!; }
    }
}
```
## Related:
Relates to test plan #76130

closes #81824

## Problem 
The current MCQ component in /learn needs to support a "speaking feature" where users can practice pronouncing specific answers. However, the existing QuizQuestion component did not support adding extra action buttons next to answers without either:

This PR refactors the QuizQuestion component to use CSS Grid with display: contents, enabling perfect visual alignment while maintaining a clean semantic DOM structure.

- renderAnswerAction Prop: Added a new optional prop that accepts a render function. This allows consumers to render custom components (like a "Practice speaking" button) next to each answer.

prs.title: Fix: onMouseDown warning for disabled items in Dropdown
prs.body:
## Issue

When a Dropdown option is disabled, React logs a warning in the console: Warning: Expected onMouseDown listener to be a function, instead got false.
This occurs because the event handler was conditionally assigned using the && operator (e.g., !disabled && handler). When the item is disabled, this expression evaluates to false, which is not a valid event handler in React.

## Fixed 

- Updated components/dropdown/Dropdown.js to use a ternary operator for the onMouseDown prop.
- Changed the logic from !item.disabled && handler to item.disabled ? undefined : handler.
- This ensures that undefined is passed when the item is disabled, which is a valid value for React event handlers and suppresses the warning.
- Confirmed usage of onMouseDown to ensure proper selection behavior (preventing premature blur events).

## Type of Change
- Bug fix

closes #1909

## PR Type:

This limitation restricts developers from running and testing Uno targets independent of other failing projects, which is particularly hindering during incremental migrations or large refactors.

- Tooling: Provides a lightweight mechanism for external tools to retrieve Uno configuration.

## Testing 🧪

issues.title: [Feature] Apply FP classifier in /scan response
issues.body:
## Summary
Integrate the trained False Positive ML model into the backend scanning pipeline to automatically predict and downrank likely false positives during live scans.

## Motivation
However, this model provides no actual value to the user until it is actively evaluating new code. By integrating the inference step directly into the scan loop, we can automatically reduce the `ml_score` of noisy findings, saving developers time and reducing alert fatigue.

## Proposed solution
1. **Create an Inference Module:** Add `backend/app/ml/fp_predictor.py` to load the trained `fp_classifier.pkl` (using `joblib` or `pickle`) and the HuggingFace `microsoft/codebert-base` tokenizer/model into memory.
2. **Hook into the Pipeline:** In `backend/app/main.py` (inside `_run_single_scan_task`), intercept the combined list of findings *before* they are saved to the database.
3. **Generate Predictions:** Extract the features (`message + file_path + rule_id`) for each finding, generate the embeddings, and pass them to the loaded classifier.
4. **Adjust Scoring:** If the model predicts a finding is a false positive (with a probability > 0.8), significantly penalize its `ml_score` (e.g., multiply the base score by 0.2) so it drops to the bottom of the user's dashboard.
5. **Fail-Safe:** Wrap the loading and inference steps in `try/except` blocks. If the `.pkl` file does not exist (e.g., on a fresh clone where training hasn't occurred yet), the scan should just skip this step and proceed normally without crashing.

## ML tier (if applicable)
- [x] Tier 1 — Triage (severity ranking, deduplication, false positive classification)
- [ ] Tier 2 — Predictive (fix success prediction, exploit scoring, pattern clustering)
- [ ] Tier 3 — Autonomous (LLM patch generation, self-healing pipeline)
- [ ] Not ML-related

## Alternatives considered
We considered completely dropping/deleting findings that the model predicts as false positives. We ruled this out because ML models are probabilistic and can make mistakes. Dropping findings silently removes the user's ability to verify them. Downranking them via the `ml_score` is much safer and maintains developer trust.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The FastAPI server gracefully starts and skips ML inference if `fp_classifier.pkl` is missing.
- [ ] When the model is present, new scans successfully pass findings through the predictor without blocking the event loop.
- [ ] Findings predicted as false positives have their `ml_score` noticeably reduced in the database.
- [ ] Tests are added to verify the graceful fallback behavior when the model is absent.

## Additional context
- Depends on the successful completion of **Issue #163 **.
- Care must be taken to ensure the CodeBERT embedding generation does not cause massive latency spikes during large scans.

issues.title: [Feature] Build false positive classifier training script
issues.body:
## Summary
Create a training script (`train_fp_classifier.py`) that builds a binary ML classifier using CodeBERT embeddings to predict whether a security finding is a false positive.

## Motivation
With user labels now being persistently collected in the database (via Issues #4.1 and #4.2), we need to operationalize this data. Training a model to automatically identify likely false positives is a core goal of the Tier 1 ML triage pipeline, which will eventually save developers significant time by filtering out noise from the scanners.

## Proposed solution
1. **Create the script:** Add `backend/scripts/train_fp_classifier.py`.
2. **Data Extraction:** Query the SQLite `findings` table for all rows where `false_positive IS NOT NULL` (labeled examples).
3. **Feature Engineering:** Concatenate the finding's `message + file_path + rule_id`. Pass this string through HuggingFace's `microsoft/codebert-base` model to extract the `[CLS]` token embedding.
4. **Model Training:** Train a `LogisticRegression` classifier on these CodeBERT embeddings. Use an 80/20 train-test split.
5. **Evaluation & Export:** Print the accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score to standard output. Serialize and save the trained model using `joblib` or `pickle` to `backend/app/ml/models/fp_classifier.pkl`.
6. **Safeguard:** Require a minimum of 50 labeled examples to run. If fewer exist, exit cleanly with a helpful message (e.g., via a `--min-samples 50` CLI argument).

## ML tier (if applicable)
- [x] Tier 1 — Triage (severity ranking, deduplication, false positive classification)
- [ ] Tier 2 — Predictive (fix success prediction, exploit scoring, pattern clustering)
- [ ] Tier 3 — Autonomous (LLM patch generation, self-healing pipeline)
- [ ] Not ML-related

## Alternatives considered
We considered fine-tuning the entire CodeBERT model end-to-end instead of just using it as a frozen feature extractor. This was ruled out for v1 because full fine-tuning requires significantly more compute (GPUs) and a much larger dataset to avoid overfitting. Training a lightweight Logistic Regression head on frozen embeddings is much faster and robust for our current data scale.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Script exits cleanly with a descriptive message if there are fewer than 50 labeled examples in the database.
- [ ] Successfully produces and saves the `fp_classifier.pkl` file when training completes.
- [ ] Prints a standard classification report (accuracy, precision, recall, F1) to `stdout` upon completion.
- [ ] `fp_classifier.pkl` (and the `models/` directory if needed) is explicitly added to `.gitignore`.

## Additional context
- Depends on the data collection foundation built in **Issue #160** and **Issue #161 **.
- HuggingFace `transformers` and `torch` (or `safetensors`) will likely need to be added to `requirements.txt`.

issues.title: [Feature] Add finding labeling endpoint
issues.body:
## Summary
Add a backend endpoint (`POST /findings/{finding_id}/label`) and database columns to allow users to persistently label scan findings as false positives or true positives.

## Motivation
To train the Tier 1 false positive classifier (CodeBERT), we need a dataset of human-labeled examples. Currently, there is no way to persistently mark a finding's validity in the database, making it impossible to collect the required training signal from user interactions.

## Proposed solution
1. **Database update:** Modify `backend/app/db.py` to add two new columns to the `findings` table: 
   - `false_positive INTEGER DEFAULT NULL` (NULL = unlabeled, 0 = true positive, 1 = false positive)
   - `labeled_at TEXT DEFAULT NULL`
2. **New Endpoint:** Create `POST /findings/{finding_id}/label` that accepts a JSON body `{ "false_positive": true | false }`. It should update the database and set `labeled_at` to the current UTC time. Return `404` if the finding ID does not exist.
3. **Read Endpoint Update:** Ensure `GET /jobs/{job_id}/findings` includes the new `false_positive` field in its returned JSON.

## ML tier (if applicable)
- [x] Tier 1 — Triage (severity ranking, deduplication, false positive classification)
- [ ] Tier 2 — Predictive (fix success prediction, exploit scoring, pattern clustering)
- [ ] Tier 3 — Autonomous (LLM patch generation, self-healing pipeline)
- [ ] Not ML-related

## Alternatives considered
We considered storing labels in a separate, dedicated `finding_labels` table to keep the core `findings` table lightweight. However, since the relationship is strictly 1:1 and the ML training scripts will query this constantly, adding the columns directly to `findings` avoids unnecessary SQL joins and simplifies the implementation.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The `POST /findings/{finding_id}/label` endpoint successfully updates the database row.
- [ ] `labeled_at` is accurately set to the current UTC time upon labeling.
- [ ] Existing `false_positive` values can be successfully overwritten (relabeling is allowed).
- [ ] The `GET /jobs/{job_id}/findings` endpoint correctly surfaces the `false_positive` field.

issues.title: Fix silent failures by checking error fields in agent execution handlers
issues.body:
**Describe the bug**
Agent handlers (e.g., LLM, MCP, Document, Email) currently hardcode `success: true` at the end of their execution blocks without inspecting the underlying API/tool response. If a dependency fails gracefully by returning a structured error object instead of throwing a hard exception (which `executor.js` would catch), the workflow engine blindly accepts it as a success. This causes downstream `condition` or `switch` nodes to branch on corrupted data with no failure ever surfacing.

**To Reproduce**
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Create a workflow utilizing an external tool step (e.g., an MCP server, Email, or Groq/OpenAI call).
2. Configure the step to fail gracefully (e.g., provide an invalid API key or bad tool parameter).
3. Execute the workflow and observe the step results.
4. See error: The step is marked as successfully completed, even though the returned payload contains a structured error object.

**Expected behavior**
Handlers should actively inspect the returned payload for an error signal (e.g., `execution.error`, `toolResult.error`, `llmRes.error`) before resolving. If an error is detected, the step should be marked as `success: false` to halt the branch and properly surface the failure.

**Screenshots**
N/A - Backend logic issue.

**Desktop (please complete the following information):**
 - OS: N/A (Backend/Node.js environment)
 - Browser: N/A
 - Version: N/A

**Smartphone (please complete the following information):**
 - Device: N/A
 - OS: N/A
 - Browser: N/A
 - Version: N/A

**Additional context**
**Affected Files & Lines:**
* `agents/handlers/llm.handler.js` (lines 65, 83)
* `agents/handlers/document.handler.js` (line 36)
* `agents/handlers/browser.handler.js` (line 13)
* `agents/handlers/mcp.handler.js` (line 22)
* `agents/handlers/tool.handler.js` (line 32)
* `agents/handlers/email.handler.js` (line 32)

**Example vulnerability (`mcp.handler.js`):**
```javascript
const execution = await invokeMcpTool({ ... });
return createStepResult({ ..., output: execution.result, success: true }); // Hardcoded success
```

**Proposed Fix:**
Standardize each handler to dynamically evaluate the error field:
```javascript
success: !execution?.error,
error: execution?.error,
```

issues.title: Runner loses state on retry, duplicating non-idempotent steps
issues.body:
**Describe the bug**
The workflow runner loses its state during task retries, causing it to duplicate non-idempotent actions (like sending emails or making HTTP calls). The fast-forward/replay logic in `runner.js` depends on `task.stepResults` to skip already-completed steps. However, when a task fails and initiates a retry, `queueService.js` completely wipes the array (`update.$set.stepResults = []`). Consequently, the runner redoes everything from scratch. Furthermore, `runner.js` does not explicitly filter out failed steps from its cache, which can cause the runner to instantly abort a retry if it loads a cached failure instead of re-executing it.

**To Reproduce**
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Create a workflow with at least two steps (e.g., Step 1: Send Slack message [Success], Step 2: API Call [Fails]).
2. Execute the workflow and wait for Step 2 to fail, putting the task into a `retrying` status.
3. Check the database/logs to observe `stepResults` being wiped to `[]`.
4. Observe the runner picking up the retry attempt.
5. See error: The runner executes Step 1 (Slack message) again instead of fast-forwarding past it, because the memory of its success was erased.

**Expected behavior**
During a retry, the system should remember previously successful steps and fast-forward past them. It should only re-execute steps that either failed or have not been attempted yet.

**Screenshots**
N/A - Backend state management issue.

**Desktop (please complete the following information):**
 - OS: N/A (Backend/Node.js environment)
 - Browser: N/A
 - Version: N/A

**Smartphone (please complete the following information):**
 - Device: N/A
 - OS: N/A
 - Browser: N/A
 - Version: N/A

**Additional context**
**Location 1:** `backend/src/agents/queueService.js` (Inside `completeTask()`)
**Location 2:** `backend/src/agents/runner.js` (Inside the branch processing cache lookup)

**Proposed Fix:**

1. In `queueService.js`, preserve successful steps in the current run array so the runner can skip them:
```javascript
update.$push.retryHistory = {
  attempt: task.attempts,
  startedAt: task.startedAt,
  failedAt: new Date(),
  error: actualError,
  stepResults: archivedSteps
};

// Fix: Wipe only the failed steps. Keep successful steps so runner can fast-forward.
update.$set.stepResults = archivedSteps.filter(res => res && res.success);
update.$set.startedAt = null;
```
1. In runner.js, explicitly ignore failed steps in the cache so it forces a re-run:

```
let result;
const cachedResult = Array.isArray(task.stepResults)
  ? task.stepResults.find(
      (r) =>
        r &&
        r.stepId === getStepId(stepNode) &&
        r.type !== 'parallel' &&
        r.type !== 'join' &&
        r.success === true // Fix: Never cache a failed step, force it to re-run
    )
  : null;

```

issues.title: file.handler.js silently overwrites files instead of deleting/listing them
issues.body: **Describe the bug** The `file.handler.js` module silently overwrites files instead of deleting or listing them. The frontend tool layer advertises 5 actions (`read`, `write`, `append`, `remove`, `list`), but the backend handler only explicitly checks for `read` and `append`. Any other action—including `remove` and `list`—falls into a catch-all `else` block that executes `fs.writeFileSync(filePath, content)`. If a user selects "Remove", the system will destroy the file's contents instead of deleting the file. **To Reproduce** Steps to reproduce the behavior: 1. Create a workflow or agent step that utilizes the file tool. 2. Set the action configuration to `remove` (or `list`) and specify a target file path. 3. Execute the workflow. 4. Check the target file on the filesystem. 5. See error: The file still exists, but its original contents have been overwritten and replaced with an empty string (or the output of the last context). **Expected behavior** When the `remove` action is passed, the handler should physically delete the file using `fs.rmSync`. When `list` is passed, it should return the directory contents. **Screenshots** N/A - Backend logic issue. **Desktop (please complete the following information):** - OS: N/A (Backend/Node.js environment) - Browser: N/A - Version: N/A **Smartphone (please complete the following information):** - Device: N/A - OS: N/A - Browser: N/A - Version: N/A **Additional context** **Location:** `backend/src/agents/handlers/file.handler.js`, lines 15-30. **Proposed Fix:** Refactor the control flow to use a strict `switch` statement that explicitly handles each supported action and returns an error for unsupported actions. ```javascript switch (config.action) { case 'read': return createStepResult({ stepId: validatedStepId, type: 'file', success: true, output: fs.readFileSync(filePath, 'utf8') }); case 'append': fs.appendFileSync(filePath, content); break; case 'write': fs.writeFileSync(filePath, content); break; case 'remove': fs.rmSync(filePath, { force: true }); break; case 'list': return createStepResult({ stepId: validatedStepId, type: 'file', success: true, output: fs.readdirSync(path.dirname(filePath)) }); default: return createStepResult({ stepId: validatedStepId, type: 'file', success: false, output: `Unsupported action: ${config.action}` }); } ```

issues.title: Trace IDs and structured execution logging (phase 4.4)
issues.body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

Currently, debugging complex, multi-step workflows is difficult because logs, errors, and metrics from concurrent workflow runs get interleaved in the console. when a specific workflow fails and we have to manually hunt through detached log lines to piece together the lifecycle of that specific execution, especially when retries and backoffs are involved.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

I would like to implement **Phase 4.4: Trace IDs and Structured Execution Logging** to finalize the Phase 4 observability roadmap (#159). 

Specifically, we should:

1. **Trace ID Generation:** Generate a unique `traceId` (e.g., UUID) at the absolute entry point of a workflow execution (the runner).
2. **Context Injection:** Inject this `traceId` into the shared execution `context` object so it automatically flows down to the dispatcher (`executor.js`) and all individual step handlers.
3. **Structured Logging:** Update the shared logging utility to automatically extract and attach this `traceId` to every structured log emitted during the run, creating a unified trace.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

* **Relying solely on `taskId` or `stepId`:** *Rejected* because these IDs don't always cover the entire operational lifecycle uniformly, especially during deep sub-agent logic or external tool invocations.
* **Ad-hoc `console.log` updates:** *Rejected* because manually appending trace IDs to every single log statement is error-prone, creates code duplication, and prevents us from achieving a clean, parsable log format for external monitoring tools.

**Additional context**
This is the final implementation step for the Phase 4 executor enhancements (#159). Once merged, the core engine will have complete retry resilience, standardized error boundaries, step-level metrics, and end-to-end lifecycle tracing.

issues.title: Step-level execution metrics and telemetry integration
issues.body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

We currently lack granular visibility into execution performance. A workflow runs slowly, but there is no step-level telemetry to tell us *which* specific handler (e.g., a slow HTTP request, a lagging LLM call, or a heavy tool execution) is causing the bottleneck. 

**Describe the solution you'd like**

I would like to implement **Phase 4.3: Step-Level Execution Metrics and Telemetry Integration** as defined in the Phase 4 architectural roadmap (#159).

Specifically, we should:
1. **Add a Timing Interceptor:** Inject a lightweight high-resolution timer (`Date.now()` or `performance.now()`) inside the centralized dispatcher (`executor.js`) wrapping the `internalExecuteStep` invocation.
2. **Payload Integration:** Calculate the `durationMs` for the step and attach it directly to the final step result payload.
*(Note: The Mongoose `StepResultSchema` in `task.model.js` already explicitly defines `durationMs` and `metrics`, so no schema updates or database migrations are required for this to work).*

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
* **Ad-hoc implementation:** Adding timers manually inside every individual handler file (`http.handler.js`, `llm.handler.js`, etc.). *Rejected* because it creates massive code duplication and violates the clean, centralized dispatcher architecture we just built.
* **External APM tools (e.g., Datadog):** *Rejected* because while good for server health, it doesn't solve the need to natively store step execution times in our own database for the frontend UI timeline.

**Additional context**

This is a direct follow-up to the Phase 4.2 PR. Implementing this centralized telemetry will give us the performance data needed before we move on to the final Phase 4.4 (Trace IDs).

issues.title: Standardized error classes and handler boundaries (phase 4.2)
issues.body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

Currently, the execution engine (`executor.js`) catches raw runtime errors and outputs them as plain text strings. This lack of structured error handling makes it difficult for the frontend, logging, or telemetry systems to programmatically parse, categorize, and gracefully display specific failure types (e.g., distinguishing between a step timeout, a validation failure, or an external API crash).

**Describe the solution you'd like**
Implement Phase 4.2 of the Executor Enhancements: Standardized error classes and handler boundaries. 

Specifically:
1. **New Error Classes:** Create a base `ExecutionError` class with specific subclasses (e.g., `TimeoutError`, `ValidationError`, `NetworkError`).
2. **Standardized Try/Catch:** Wrap the handler execution within `executor.js` in a standardized boundary that normalizes all thrown errors before they bubble up to the main runner.
3. **Data Model Changes:** Update the step execution result object to return a uniform error payload containing a standardized `code`, `message`, and `details/stack` format.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
An alternative is leaving the errors as raw strings and attempting to parse or regex them on the frontend to figure out what went wrong. However, this is highly fragile, difficult to scale as new nodes are added, and leads to poor UX. 

**Additional context**
This represents Phase 4.2 of the overarching Phase 4 Executor Enhancements tracking issue (#159).

issues.title: Feature: Phase 4 Executor Enhancements (Retries, Metrics, Error Handling, Tracing)
issues.body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

While the recent architectural refactor successfully modularized `executor.js` into dedicated handlers, the execution pipeline lacks a few production-grade resilience and observability features. Currently, transient network failures can cause immediate step failure (lack of retries), performance bottlenecks are hard to pinpoint (lack of step-level metrics), error reporting can be inconsistent across different handlers, and debugging complex workflows is difficult without a unified trace.

**Describe the solution you'd like**

I would like to implement the "Phase 4" enhancements discussed during the core refactor. Specifically, we should enhance the lightweight dispatcher and utility modules with:

1. **Retry Policies:** Configurable retry logic (e.g., exponential backoff) for steps prone to transient errors, such as external API calls.
2. **Step-Level Metrics:** Telemetry to record execution duration, success/failure rates, and throughput for individual step handlers.
3. **Standardized Error Handling:** A unified error class hierarchy and a consistent error-boundary wrapper around all handlers to ensure predictable failure modes and logging.
4. **Execution Tracing:** Unique trace IDs and structured logging that span the entire lifecycle of a workflow execution to improve debuggability.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

* **Ad-hoc implementation:** Implementing retries and logging manually inside each specific handler. *Rejected because it leads to code duplication and violates the clean architecture established in the recent refactor.*
* **Third-party workflow engines:** Migrating to a heavy workflow engine like Temporal or Step Functions. *Rejected because it's overkill for our current needs; we can achieve the necessary resilience by enhancing our current lightweight dispatcher.*

**Additional context**

This is a direct follow-up to the `executor.js` modularization PR.

issues.title: [Bug] localStorage QuotaExceededError due to full ScanResponse serialization
issues.body:
## What happened
The frontend attempts to write the entire `ScanResponse` object—including thousands of individual findings—directly into the browser's `localStorage`. This easily breaches the strict 5 MB storage limit in modern browsers, causing a silent `QuotaExceededError` that breaks state persistence across the app.

## Steps to reproduce
1. Run a repository scan on a moderately large project that generates thousands of findings (e.g., >1,500).
2. Wait for the scan to complete and the frontend to trigger the `saveLastScan` function in `frontend/src/app/lib/scan-store.ts`.
3. Open the browser's developer tools console and look for a `QuotaExceededError`.
4. Navigate to another page in the app (like the Dashboard or Reports page) or refresh the browser.
5. Observe that the app treats your session as if "no scan" has occurred because the save silently failed.

## Expected behaviour
The application should persist only lightweight session markers (such as `job_id`, `project_name`, and basic metadata) in `localStorage`. Heavy data like the findings array should be fetched dynamically from the backend (`GET /jobs/{job_id}/findings`) when needed, ideally with pagination.

## Actual behaviour
The `saveLastScan` function serializes the entire JSON blob. When it hits the 5 MB wall, the browser blocks the write operation, leaving downstream pages without the required state.

## Environment

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| OS | All |
| Python version | N/A |
| Node version | N/A |
| PatchPilot version / commit | main |
| Scan method | Any scan producing a large number of findings |

## Logs

```text
N/A - This throws a silent QuotaExceededError in the browser console.
```
