issues.title: [Bug] Unvalidated github_username leads to database bloat and DoS (Leaderboard Spam)
issues.body:
## What happened
The `/leaderboard/update` endpoint does not validate the length or format of the `github_username` field. An attacker can submit massive junk strings (e.g., megabytes of text) as their username, bloating the database and causing a Denial of Service (DoS) when the leaderboard is queried.

## Steps to reproduce
1. Construct a POST request to the `/leaderboard/update` endpoint.
2. Set the `github_username` field to an excessively large payload (e.g., 1,000,000 characters of 'A').
3. Submit the request.
4. Query the `/leaderboard` endpoint and observe the bloated payload being returned, causing high memory usage and slow response times.

## Expected behaviour
The API should strictly validate the `github_username` against GitHub's actual username constraints (alphanumeric with single hyphens, maximum 39 characters) before touching the database. Invalid requests should be rejected immediately with a 422 Unprocessable Entity or 400 Bad Request error.

## Actual behaviour
The server accepts the unvalidated payload and writes it directly to the database. Subsequent queries to the leaderboard load this massive string into memory and transmit it to clients, degrading system performance.

## Environment

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| OS | Ubuntu 22.04 LTS |
| Python version | 3.11.4 |
| Node version | 18.17.0 |
| PatchPilot version / commit | main @ 9b4d1f2 |
| Scan method | N/A (Leaderboard API) |

## Logs

```text
INFO:     192.168.1.10:60122 - "POST /leaderboard/update HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
INFO:     192.168.1.15:60123 - "GET /leaderboard HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
WARNING:  High memory usage detected formatting JSON response (145MB allocated).
```

## Additional context
This can be easily fixed by adding Pydantic field validations (e.g., `Field(max_length=39, pattern=...)`) to the `LeaderboardUpdateRequest` model.

issues.title: [Bug] Unbounded API calls on GitHub fetch lead to DoS (No Timeout)
issues.body:
## What happened
Fetching GitHub repositories or organization data via the API does not enforce strict timeouts. If the upstream GitHub server hangs or drops packets without closing the connection, our backend worker thread will hang indefinitely, leading to resource exhaustion.

## Steps to reproduce
1. Spin up a mock HTTP server that accepts connections but never sends data (e.g., a simple slow-loris or `nc -l 8080`).
2. Intercept an outbound request from the backend (like an org scan or repo fetch) and route it to this mock server, or artificially throttle the network connection to 0 bytes/sec mid-stream.
3. Observe the backend worker thread hanging forever without timing out or releasing system resources.

## Expected behaviour
All outbound HTTP requests (e.g., using `httpx` or `requests`) must be configured with explicit connect and read timeouts (e.g., 10-30 seconds). If the timeout is exceeded, the request should fail gracefully, returning a 408 Request Timeout or 504 Gateway Timeout, freeing up the worker thread.

## Actual behaviour
The worker thread remains blocked indefinitely waiting for data that will never arrive. Under load, this can exhaust all available worker threads and freeze the entire application.

## Environment

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| OS | Ubuntu 22.04 LTS |
| Python version | 3.11.4 |
| Node version | 18.17.0 |
| PatchPilot version / commit | main @ 9b4d1f2 |
| Scan method | GitHub URL / Org Scan |

## Logs

```text
INFO:     192.168.1.5:54321 - "POST /api/scans/org HTTP/1.1" 202 Accepted
INFO:     Fetching organization repositories from GitHub API...
WARNING:  Worker thread blocked for > 300 seconds.
WARNING:  Thread pool exhaustion detected.
```

## Additional context
Standard Python HTTP libraries (`httpx`, `requests`) do not enforce a default timeout unless explicitly passed. We need to audit all outbound `client.get()` calls and ensure a `timeout=` parameter is globally applied.

issues.title: [Bug] Unbounded file downloads on /scan-url cause Denial of Service (DoS)
issues.body:
## What happened
The `/scan-url` endpoint does not enforce size limits when downloading external resources. Supplying a URL that points to a massive file or an infinite data stream will cause the backend to download it indefinitely, crashing the server.

## Steps to reproduce
1. Spin up a public server hosting an infinite byte stream (e.g., reading from `/dev/urandom`) or a massive multi-gigabyte file.
2. Submit this URL to the Patch Pilot `/scan-url` endpoint.
3. Monitor the disk and memory usage of the backend infrastructure.

## Expected behaviour
The application should stream the download in manageable chunks and verify the accumulated size against a strict threshold (e.g., 50MB). If the threshold is breached, the download should be immediately terminated and the user should receive an HTTP 400 or 413 error.

## Actual behaviour
The application attempts to download the entire file without restriction. This rapidly consumes all available RAM or disk space, eventually bringing the backend service down entirely.

## Environment

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| OS | Ubuntu 22.04 LTS |
| Python version | 3.11.4 |
| Node version | 18.17.0 |
| PatchPilot version / commit | main @ e4a2b1c |
| Scan method | GitHub URL |

## Logs

```text
INFO:     192.168.1.15:60122 - "POST /api/v1/scan-url HTTP/1.1" 202 Accepted
INFO:     Fetching repository from remote URL...
ERROR:    Exception in worker task: MemoryError
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/app/main.py", line 142, in download_repo
    data = response.read()
MemoryError
```

## Additional context
This is a classic Denial of Service (DoS) vector. Even if a timeout is implemented, a fast connection could still allow gigabytes of data to be downloaded in a few seconds. A strict byte-limit cutoff is required on the download stream.

issues.title: [Bug] RAM exhaustion via spoofed Content-Length headers on /upload
issues.body:
## What happened
The application trusts the user-provided `Content-Length` header during file uploads instead of calculating the actual bytes streamed. An attacker can send a massive payload with a deceptively small `Content-Length` header, leading to memory exhaustion and server crashes.

## Steps to reproduce
1. Intercept a legitimate file upload request to the backend using a tool like Burp Suite or Postman.
2. Modify the `Content-Length` header to a small, acceptable value (e.g., `Content-Length: 1024`).
3. Replace the request body with a massive data stream (e.g., several gigabytes of junk data).
4. Send the request and monitor the backend server's RAM usage.

## Expected behaviour
The server should stream the upload in chunks and maintain an internal byte counter. If the actual bytes received exceed the maximum allowed upload size limit, the server should immediately abort the connection and return an HTTP 413 Payload Too Large error, regardless of what the `Content-Length` header claims.

## Actual behaviour
The server continues to read the incoming stream into memory based on the bypassed header checks. RAM usage spikes until the operating system's OOM (Out of Memory) killer terminates the application.

## Environment

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| OS | Ubuntu 22.04 LTS |
| Python version | 3.11.4 |
| Node version | 18.17.0 |
| PatchPilot version / commit | main @ e4a2b1c |
| Scan method | ZIP upload |

## Logs

```text
INFO:     192.168.1.10:54321 - "POST /api/v1/upload HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
WARNING:  High memory usage detected (85%)
WARNING:  High memory usage detected (95%)
Killed
[uvicorn] worker process 4012 exited with code 137 (OOM Killed)
```

## Additional context
This bypasses standard validation middleware that relies purely on headers. A robust fix requires implementing a streaming read mechanism with a hard byte-count cutoff at the application layer.

issues.title: [Bug] Severe CORS Misconfiguration: Wildcard Origins with Credentials
issues.body:
## What happened
The FastAPI CORS middleware is configured to allow all origins (`allow_origins=["*"]`) while simultaneously allowing credentials (`allow_credentials=True`). This exposes the API to massive cross-origin data leaks.

## Steps to reproduce
1. Inspect the FastAPI initialization in `app/main.py`.
2. Observe the middleware configuration: `allow_origins=["*"]` is combined with `allow_credentials=True`.
3. A malicious third-party website can make authenticated API requests to the PatchPilot backend on behalf of a user and read sensitive vulnerability data in the response.

## Expected behaviour
If `allow_credentials=True` is required for session management or cookies, `allow_origins` must be strictly defined to trusted frontend domains (e.g., `["http://localhost:5173", "https://app.patchpilot.com"]`).

## Actual behaviour
The API indiscriminately trusts all origins with credentials, effectively neutralizing Same-Origin Policy (SOP) protections.

## Environment

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| OS | Any |
| Python version | 3.10+ |
| Node version | N/A |
| PatchPilot version / commit | main |
| Scan method | N/A |

## Logs

```python
# Code snippet from app/main.py causing the issue:
app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origins=["*"],
    allow_credentials=True,
    allow_methods=["*"],
    allow_headers=["*"],
)
```
## Additional context

Modern browsers often block literal wildcard origins (*) when credentials are included, but depending on how the backend framework dynamically resolves the wildcard (often by simply echoing the incoming malicious Origin header back to the client), it bypasses browser protections entirely. This allows attackers to steal session data or sensitive scan reports.

issues.title: [Bug]  Zip Slip: Arbitrary File Overwrite via Unsafe ZIP Extraction
issues.body:
## What happened
The `/scan` endpoint extracts uploaded ZIP files without validating the internal file paths. A malicious ZIP archive containing directory traversal sequences (e.g., `../../`) can overwrite core server files outside the intended working directory.

## Steps to reproduce
1. Create a malicious ZIP file containing a payload file with a relative path like `../../../../app/main.py`.
2. Go to the PatchPilot dashboard and upload the malicious ZIP via the "Start New Scan" feature.
3. The backend extracts the archive using `unzip_to_dir` without sanitizing the paths, overwriting the target file on the host filesystem.

## Expected behaviour
The extraction utility (`unzip_to_dir`) should validate all paths inside the ZIP archive. If any path attempts to resolve outside the designated temporary `WORK_ROOT` directory, the extraction should be aborted and an HTTP 400 error returned.

## Actual behaviour
The backend blindly trusts the archive's relative file paths and writes them directly to the filesystem, resulting in an arbitrary file overwrite.

## Environment

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| OS | Any |
| Python version | 3.10+ |
| Node version | N/A |
| PatchPilot version / commit | main |
| Scan method | ZIP upload |

## Logs

```text
INFO:     127.0.0.1:52134 - "POST /scan HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
# Silent file overwrite occurs during unzip_to_dir execution.
```

## Additional context

This is a critical security vulnerability known as "Zip Slip". Overwriting core application files (like main.py or .env) can immediately lead to Remote Code Execution (RCE) or complete server compromise.

issues.title: [Feature] Add Advanced Filtering, Global Search, and Remediation Routing to Blast Radius Graph
issues.body:
## Summary
Implement interactive severity filtering, global package search, aggregate metrics, and actionable remediation routing to make the blast radius graph scalable and actionable for enterprise environments.

## Motivation
While the graph beautifully illustrates structural context, enterprise organizations often have thousands of dependencies. Visualizing all of them simultaneously creates cognitive overload. Security engineers need to instantly filter noise to focus on high-priority targets (e.g., Critical vulnerabilities), search for specific packages across the network, and seamlessly transition from visual diagnosis to actual remediation without losing context.

## Proposed solution
We will upgrade the `BlastRadiusGraph` component with four major capabilities:
1. **Filter Controls:** Pill-shaped toggle filters (e.g., "Vulnerable", "Secure") overlaid on the canvas.
2. **Global Search:** A sleek text input overlay to instantly locate a specific package by ID.
3. **Ghosting Engine:** Instead of deleting nodes from the data array (which breaks the physics layout), nodes that don't match the search or filter criteria will drop to `0.02` opacity and lose their hitboxes, preserving the structural map while removing visual noise.
4. **Aggregate Empty State:** When no node is selected, the detail panel will display macro-metrics (Total visible nodes, Total vulnerable visible nodes).
5. **Remediation Action:** A "Remediate" button in the active detail panel that uses React Router to navigate directly to the fix workflow for the selected vulnerability.

## ML tier (if applicable)
- [ ] Tier 1 — Triage (severity ranking, deduplication, false positive classification)
- [ ] Tier 2 — Predictive (fix success prediction, exploit scoring, pattern clustering)
- [ ] Tier 3 — Autonomous (LLM patch generation, self-healing pipeline)
- [x] Not ML-related

## Alternatives considered
- **Backend Filtering:** Ruled out because requiring a new API call for every filter click introduces unacceptable latency. In-memory WebGL opacity filtering is instantaneous.
- **Removing Nodes from Physics Engine:** Ruled out. Dynamically slicing the `data` array forces `d3-force` to recalculate the layout, causing the graph to violently collapse and rebuild, destroying the user's mental map of the architecture.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Filter toggles and the search bar successfully update local state.
- [ ] The `nodeCanvasObject` and `linkColor` functions accurately evaluate filter/search state and "ghost" non-matching elements without altering the physics layout.
- [ ] The detail panel renders an "Aggregate View" displaying current visible node counts when no node is actively selected.
- [ ] The detail panel renders an action button (for vulnerable nodes only) that successfully routes the user to the remediation view via React Router.

## Additional context
This is Part 4 of 4 for the "Cross-Repo Blast Radius Graph" initiative, finalizing the visualization tool into a complete, actionable product workflow.

issues.title: [Feature] Implement interactive node selection, visual path highlighting
issues.body:
## Summary
Implement interactive node selection, visual path highlighting, and a contextual detail panel for deep-dive vulnerability inspection within the blast radius graph.

## Motivation
A static network graph beautifully illustrates the structural complexity of an organization, but security engineers need actionable insights. Currently, the user can see which nodes are vulnerable, but they cannot easily see *what* the vulnerability is or *which* specific repositories rely on that compromised package without cross-referencing the table below. Adding interactivity transforms the graph from a pure visualization into a functional diagnostic tool.

## Proposed solution
We will upgrade the `BlastRadiusGraph` component with a selection state engine.
1. **On Node Click:** The camera smoothly auto-centers on the clicked node.
2. **Path Highlighting:** All unconnected nodes and links fade into the background. The selected node, its immediate connections, and the directional dependency paths remain bright.
3. **Contextual Overlay:** A sleek, semi-transparent side panel slides in over the graph canvas displaying the node's ID, type, and specific security findings associated with it. 
4. **On Background Click:** The selection clears, the panel slides out, and the graph returns to its default global view.

## ML tier (if applicable)
- [ ] Tier 1 — Triage (severity ranking, deduplication, false positive classification)
- [ ] Tier 2 — Predictive (fix success prediction, exploit scoring, pattern clustering)
- [ ] Tier 3 — Autonomous (LLM patch generation, self-healing pipeline)
- [x] Not ML-related

## Alternatives considered
- **Hover Tooltips:** We considered rendering data on mouse hover. This was ruled out because tooltips often clip off-screen near canvas edges, and hover states make it difficult for users to copy/paste CVE numbers or file paths. A dedicated click-to-lock side panel is much more robust.
- **Navigating to a new page:** Ruled out to maintain the single-page application workflow and prevent users from losing their structural context.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Clicking a node successfully updates the local selection state and triggers a camera zoom/center event on that node.
- [ ] The `nodeCanvasObject` and `linkColor` logic dynamically updates to dim unselected, unconnected graph elements.
- [ ] A contextual detail panel renders inside the graph container when a node is selected, displaying relevant node data.
- [ ] Clicking the canvas background (empty space) successfully clears the selection and restores the default visual state.

## Additional context
This is Part 3 of the 4-part "Cross-Repo Blast Radius Graph" initiative. It builds directly upon the WebGL engine.

issues.title: [Feature] frontend UI for the cross-repo blast radius
issues.body:
## Summary
Implement the frontend UI for the cross-repo blast radius by visualizing the organization's dependency data as an interactive 2D graph.

## Motivation
Now that the backend successfully maps cross-repo dependency connections, users need a visual way to explore this data. Staring at raw JSON payloads or flat tables does not effectively convey the structural impact of a vulnerability. A node-link graph makes it instantly clear how microservices and shared packages are connected, allowing security teams to immediately understand the architectural spread.

## Proposed solution
We will build a new React component (e.g., `BlastRadiusGraph.jsx`) that fetches the payload from the newly created `GET /api/scans/org/{org_job_id}/blast-radius` endpoint. 

To handle enterprise-scale node counts without crashing the browser, we will integrate a WebGL-powered physics graph library (like `react-force-graph-2d`). The surrounding container will be styled cleanly with standard Tailwind CSS. To maintain a high-end, minimalist aesthetic, nodes will be visually distinct based on their type:
- Dark/Slate nodes for Repositories.
- Lighter/Gray nodes for standard Packages.
- Stark Crimson/Red nodes for vulnerable Packages.

*Note: This issue focuses purely on the rendering and layout. Deep interaction (on-click filtering, sidebars) will be handled in Part 3.*

## ML tier (if applicable)
- [ ] Tier 1 — Triage (severity ranking, deduplication, false positive classification)
- [ ] Tier 2 — Predictive (fix success prediction, exploit scoring, pattern clustering)
- [ ] Tier 3 — Autonomous (LLM patch generation, self-healing pipeline)
- [x] Not ML-related

## Alternatives considered
We considered building a custom SVG-based graph using pure D3.js. This was ruled out because standard SVG DOM elements struggle with performance when scaling to hundreds or thousands of nodes (which is common in large organization dependency trees). A Canvas/WebGL approach provides significantly better physics and rendering performance.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The `react-force-graph-2d` (or equivalent WebGL library) is successfully installed and configured in the Vite project.
- [ ] A new React component successfully fetches and parses data from the `/blast-radius` API endpoint.
- [ ] The graph renders visually on the dashboard, correctly mapping the `"nodes"` and `"links"` arrays.
- [ ] Nodes are distinctly color-coded based on their type (`repo`, `package`) and security status (`vulnerable: true/false`).

## Additional context
This is Part 2 of the 4-part "Cross-Repo Blast Radius Graph" initiative. It directly consumes the API built and merged in Part 1.

issues.title: [Feature] feat: implement backend dependency parsing and blast radius API
issues.body:
## Summary
Implement backend dependency parsing and create a new API endpoint to expose an organizational adjacency graph for cross-repo blast radius mapping.

## Motivation
To visually map a vulnerability's impact across an entire organization, the backend must first understand the architecture. Currently, vulnerabilities are siloed per repository; security teams have no automated way to see if a compromised shared package is imported by 50 other internal microservices. Generating a structural dependency graph unlocks the ability to track a vulnerability's true "blast radius."

## Proposed solution
1. Update the Python batch scanning engine to identify and parse dependency manifests (starting with `package.json` and `requirements.txt`) during the ingestion phase.
2. Store the dependency relationships in a lightweight adjacency list structure (e.g., mapping `source_repo` -> `target_dependency`).
3. Create a new FastAPI endpoint (`GET /api/scans/org/{org_id}/blast-radius`) that serializes this relational data and returns a standard graph JSON payload containing `"nodes"` and `"links"` arrays.

## ML tier (if applicable)
- [ ] Tier 1 — Triage (severity ranking, deduplication, false positive classification)
- [ ] Tier 2 — Predictive (fix success prediction, exploit scoring, pattern clustering)
- [ ] Tier 3 — Autonomous (LLM patch generation, self-healing pipeline)
- [x] Not ML-related

## Alternatives considered
We considered implementing a dedicated Graph Database (like Neo4j) to store and traverse the nodes. This was ruled out because the infrastructure overhead is too high for our current scale; standard relational adjacency lists or in-memory graph construction will be highly performant for typical enterprise dependency trees.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The scanning engine successfully extracts and records dependencies from at least one manifest type (e.g., `package.json`) during an organization batch scan.
- [ ] A new `GET /api/scans/org/{org_id}/blast-radius` endpoint is reachable and returns a valid JSON structure containing `nodes` and `links`.
- [ ] The endpoint implements proper authorization checks to ensure users cannot retrieve graph architectures for organizations they do not belong to.

## Additional context
This is Part 1 of the 4-part "Cross-Repo Blast Radius Graph" initiative. This PR establishes the data pipeline, which will immediately be consumed by the frontend WebGL visualization in Part 2.

issues.title: [Feature] Organization-Wide PDF Audit Reports
issues.body:
## Summary
Implements organization-wide PDF audit report generation to export aggregated security findings across all scanned repositories.

## Motivation
While the new Enterprise Dashboard provides a great real-time view of an organization's security posture, compliance teams, auditors, and executives require a consolidated, offline artifact. Currently, users can only generate PDF reports for single repositories, making organization-wide compliance reporting a manual and tedious process.

## Proposed solution
* **Backend API:** Create a new endpoint `GET /api/scans/org/{org_job_id}/report/pdf` that fetches the aggregated organization data.
* **PDF Generator:** Extend the existing `pdf_builder.py` (or create an `org_pdf_builder.py`) to handle multi-repository data. The layout will include the aggregate scorecards, severity distribution, top vulnerable repositories, and a combined ledger of findings.
* **Frontend UI:** Add an "Export Report (PDF)" button to the header of the `OrgFindings.tsx` dashboard. It will manage the downloading state and trigger the file save directly to the user's machine.

## ML tier (if applicable)
- [ ] Tier 1 — Triage (severity ranking, deduplication, false positive classification)
- [ ] Tier 2 — Predictive (fix success prediction, exploit scoring, pattern clustering)
- [ ] Tier 3 — Autonomous (LLM patch generation, self-healing pipeline)
- [x] Not ML-related

## Alternatives considered
* **CSV/Excel Export:** Considered for raw data dumping, but ruled out for this phase because PDF is the industry standard for immutable, executive-ready compliance artifacts. We can add CSV later if requested.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The new `/org/{org_job_id}/report/pdf` endpoint successfully generates and returns a valid PDF file.
- [ ] The generated PDF accurately reflects organization-level metrics (total repos, aggregate severity, multi-repo findings) rather than single-repo data.
- [ ] An "Export PDF" button is present and functional on the `OrgFindings` dashboard.
- [ ] The UI displays a proper loading state (e.g., spinner) while the heavy PDF generation process runs on the backend.

## Additional context
The styling of the Organization PDF should closely mirror the existing single-repo PDF to maintain consistent product branding, just adapted for cross-repository data tables.

issues.title: [Feature] Smart Scan Resilience & Org-Wide Analytics Dashboard(Phase-3.1)
issues.body:
## Summary
Implements an enterprise-grade resilience layer for GitHub organization batch scanning and exposes aggregated organizational security intelligence via new summary and findings APIs.

## Motivation
Currently, scanning large organizations (50+ repositories) triggers GitHub secondary rate limits (403/429), causing partial scan failures. Additionally, findings are siloed per-repository; users currently have to aggregate security posture manually, which is a significant friction point for enterprise compliance audits.

## Proposed solution
* **Backend Resilience:** Introduce an exponential backoff/jitter retry mechanism into the repository ZIP downloader to gracefully handle GitHub API throttling.
* **Aggregation APIs:** Introduce `GET /api/scans/org/{org_job_id}/summary` (for high-level posture metrics) and `GET /api/scans/org/{org_job_id}/findings` (for flattened, cross-repo data).
* **Enterprise Dashboard:** A new React route `OrgFindings.tsx` featuring visual aggregate charts (Severity/CWE distribution) and a filterable, global table for sorting vulnerabilities across the entire organizational scan.

## ML tier (if applicable)
- [ ] Tier 1 — Triage (severity ranking, deduplication, false positive classification)
- [ ] Tier 2 — Predictive (fix success prediction, exploit scoring, pattern clustering)
- [ ] Tier 3 — Autonomous (LLM patch generation, self-healing pipeline)
- [x] Not ML-related

## Alternatives considered
* **Worker Queue (Celery/Redis):** Considered for managing background tasks, but ruled out to maintain simplicity and avoid adding infrastructure complexity until strictly necessary.
* **Database Migration to PostgreSQL:** Considered for solving SQLite locking, but decided to keep SQLite for now, leveraging per-tick connection management and retry loops to stay within project scope.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] GitHub scans complete successfully without crashing despite temporary API rate limits.
- [ ] New `OrgFindings` dashboard correctly renders top-vulnerable repositories and vulnerability counts.
- [ ] Findings table successfully filters and sorts across all repositories in the batch job.
- [ ] "View Collected Analytics" button accurately routes users from the Scan Modal to the new dashboard.

## Additional context

The backend aggregation strategy leverages grouped SQL queries to compute metrics in a single pass, ensuring the UI remains snappy even with thousands of individual findings.

issues.title: [Feature] SSE Real-Time Tracking & Abort Controls for Batch Scans
issues.body:
## Summary
Replace short-polling with Server-Sent Events (SSE) for high-performance scan tracking and introduce an abort mechanism (circuit breaker) to cancel ongoing organization batch scans.

## Motivation
Currently, the batch scanning UI relies on a 3-second `setInterval` polling loop. While functional, this hammers the SQLite database, introduces UI latency, and scales poorly under high load. Additionally, once a massive organization scan is initiated, the user has no way to halt it. If an organization has hundreds of repositories, a runaway scan can exhaust server resources, block the semaphore queue, and trigger GitHub API rate limits. Users need explicit control to cancel these heavy operations mid-flight.

## Proposed solution
**Backend (FastAPI):**
1. Create a `GET /api/scans/org/{org_job_id}/stream` endpoint using `StreamingResponse` to push JSON-formatted SSE chunks to the client.
2. Implement a circuit breaker via a new `POST /api/scans/org/{org_job_id}/abort` endpoint that updates the database status to `aborted`.
3. Update the `_run_repo_scan_task` worker loop to explicitly check the database for the `aborted` flag *before* processing the next repository, ensuring a clean and immediate exit.

**Frontend (React):**
1. Gut the `setInterval` logic in `dashboard.tsx` and replace it with the native browser `EventSource` API.
2. Add a destructive "Cancel Scan" button to the tracking modal footer (visible only during active scans).
3. Update the modal UI to visually reflect an `aborted` state (e.g., rose/red palette) and cleanly close the `EventSource` connection on completion, abortion, or component unmount to prevent memory leaks.

## ML tier (if applicable)
- [ ] Tier 1 — Triage (severity ranking, deduplication, false positive classification)
- [ ] Tier 2 — Predictive (fix success prediction, exploit scoring, pattern clustering)
- [ ] Tier 3 — Autonomous (LLM patch generation, self-healing pipeline)
- [x] Not ML-related

## Alternatives considered
- **WebSockets:** Considered for real-time updates, but ruled out as overkill. The data flow is strictly unidirectional (server to client), making Server-Sent Events (SSE) the lighter, more native, and more efficient choice.
- **Keeping the Polling Loop:** Ruled out due to unnecessary database read load and suboptimal UX (status flickering between 3-second intervals).

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The frontend receives live status updates via an SSE stream without making repeated HTTP requests.
- [ ] Users can click a "Cancel Scan" button that immediately halts the background worker loop on the backend.
- [ ] The backend async generator explicitly opens and closes its `aiosqlite` database connections per tick to prevent the `RuntimeError: threads can only be started once` exhaustion bug.
- [ ] The UI successfully renders an `aborted` state and `EventSource` connections are cleanly terminated when the component unmounts.

## Additional context
This is Phase 2 of the multi-repo scanning feature.

issues.title: [Feature] multi-repo batch scan engine & real-time polling UI (Phase 1)
issues.body:
## Summary
Introduce a multi-repo batch scanning engine that allows users to scan an entire GitHub Organization concurrently while tracking live repository-by-repository progress on a dedicated polling dashboard.

## Motivation
Currently, users can only scan a single repository URL or upload a individual ZIP file at a time. For engineering managers or security teams overseeing multiple microservices or monorepos under an organization, importing repositories one by one is highly inefficient, repetitive, and introduces substantial friction to the workflow.

## Proposed solution
We will implement an isolated "Scan Organization" workflow separate from the single-repo scan interfaces to eliminate potential merge conflicts with ongoing real-time updates (like Issue #72). 

From the user's perspective:
1. A new **"Scan Organization"** button will be added to the main Dashboard workspace next to the existing ZIP/URL options.
2. Clicking it opens a modal requesting a GitHub Organization URL.
3. Upon submission, the UI routes to a dedicated `<BatchScanProgress />` screen showing a list of all detected public repositories under that organization.
4. The screen will utilize standard HTTP short-polling (every 3 seconds) to pull the latest statuses from the backend, moving each repository through visual lifecycle states (e.g., pending clock icon, active spinning loader during scanning, green checkmarks upon completion, or explicit error markers).
5. Once all repositories hit a terminal state, a button appears directing the user to the core `<Findings />` page, updated with a new "Repository" filter column so they can isolate vulnerabilities by project.

From the technical/API perspective:
1. **Database Schema:** Create an `org_jobs` table to track the master transaction (`org_job_id`, `org_name`, `status`). Add an `org_job_id` foreign key column to the existing `jobs` table to group child repository runs.
2. **GitHub API integration:** Create a service utility to safely parse organization structures from `https://api.github.com/orgs/{org}/repos`. It will securely read an environment variable `GITHUB_PAT` (Personal Access Token) to prevent hitting unauthenticated rate limits (bumping limits from 60 requests/hour to 5,000 requests/hour).
3. **Endpoint 1 (`POST /api/scans/org`):** Validates the org URL structure against SSRF targets, initializes rows, and spawns parallel tasks.
4. **Concurrency Guardrail:** Inside the backend orchestrator, process the repository scans under an `asyncio.Semaphore(5)` lock. Capping concurrent scans at a maximum of 5 prevents high-utilization tools like Semgrep/OSV from overwhelming system memory and causing Out-Of-Memory (OOM) app crashes.
5. **Endpoint 2 (`GET /api/scans/org/{org_job_id}/status`):** Returns a clean JSON payload mapping the high-level states of all associated child jobs.

## ML tier (if applicable)
- [ ] Tier 1 — Triage (severity ranking, deduplication, false positive classification)
- [ ] Tier 2 — Predictive (fix success prediction, exploit scoring, pattern clustering)
- [ ] Tier 3 — Autonomous (LLM patch generation, self-healing pipeline)
- [x] Not ML-related

## Alternatives considered
- **Overloading the single `/scan` endpoint:** Ruled out because mixing bulk workflows into single tracking tables introduces significant architectural noise and breaks isolated testing paths.
- **Running unlimited scans simultaneously:** Ruled out because simultaneous cloning and parallel processing of 20+ microservices will instantly deplete host RAM resources.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Inputting a valid GitHub Organization URL fetches all public repositories using a authenticated PAT and correctly registers the parent-child relational database keys.
- [ ] Background tasks process up to 5 concurrent repository scans safely using a semaphore lock without blocking the API or crashing the application instance.
- [ ] The `<BatchScanProgress />` frontend component runs clean 3-second short-polling queries, transitions loading states dynamically, and safely stops the polling mechanism with proper interval clearings on component unmount.
- [ ] Clicking to view findings opens the existing `<Findings />` view displaying all collected vulnerabilities with an added "Repository" filtering column.

## Additional context
Reuses the exact underlying `_scan_repo_dir` scanning routine to prevent duplication of parsing logic.

issues.title: [Feature] Implement cryptographic PDF compliance report generator from evidence packs
issues.body:
## Summary
This feature adds a backend service and corresponding API endpoint to compile raw, ML-triaged evidence packs into an unmodifiable, audit-ready PDF document complete with an embedded cryptographic signature.

## Motivation
Raw JSON data streams and frontend dashboards are valuable for interactive remediation, but enterprise compliance teams require immutable, official documentation for compliance proofs. Currently, developers have to capture manual screenshots or parse raw payloads to generate compliance reports. Providing an automated, verifiable document stream speeds up compliance pipelines and increases platform trust.

## Proposed solution
* **Reporting Core:** Integrate `FPDF2` within our environment configuration files to handle high-performance, low-memory PDF generation directly from memory byte buffers.
* **Data Mapping:** Build a layout engine that ingests the authenticated ML-ranked evidence pack JSON and translates it into an organized, minimalist design layout.
* **Signing Flow:** Leverage a lightweight signing mechanism to seal the output byte array with a verification key, generating a valid, cryptographically verified document stream before transport.

## ML tier (if applicable)
- [ ] Tier 1 — Triage (severity ranking, deduplication, false positive classification)
- [ ] Tier 2 — Predictive (fix success prediction, exploit scoring, pattern clustering)
- [ ] Tier 3 — Autonomous (LLM patch generation, self-healing pipeline)
- [x] Not ML-related

## Alternatives considered
* **Headless Browser Rendering (Playwright/Puppeteer to PDF):** Rejected. Running headless browsers within minimal cloud environments or production runners introduces massive memory overhead, requires extensive OS-level dependencies, and complicates the direct byte-level signing flow.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Exposed API endpoint handles incoming authenticated evidence pack payloads and renders an structured PDF byte stream.
- [ ] Output PDF contains a valid, verifiable embedded cryptographic signature verifying document integrity.
- [ ] File generation code runs cleanly without creating or leaving temporary un-tracked file artifacts on cloud runners.
- [ ] Report generation logic includes comprehensive Pytest coverage mocking the byte stream to protect the 35% minimum pipeline testing threshold.

issues.title: feat(scanner): Extend core execution engine to support IaC, Dockerfiles, and GitHub Actions workflows
issues.body:
## Summary
This feature extends PatchPilot's automated vulnerability scanning engine to discover and ingest security misconfigurations within Dockerfiles, Terraform definitions, and GitHub Actions workflows.

## Motivation
Currently, PatchPilot only tracks application-level package vulnerabilities and hardcoded secrets. This narrow focus leaves critical deployment infrastructure exposed. Infrastructure as Code (IaC) bugs—such as running Docker containers with root privileges, overly permissive cloud security groups, or unpinned third-party actions—can bypass our current defenses. Manually reviewing these configuration files wastes development cycles and risks environment parity drift between local environments and cloud targets.

## Proposed solution
* **Scanner Expansion:** Update our core execution module to append native Semgrep registries: `p/dockerfile`, `p/terraform`, and `p/github-actions`.
* **Discovery Layer:** Modify file pre-filtering logic to stop ignoring configuration files, ensuring `.tf`, `Dockerfile`, and `.github/workflows/*.yml` paths are actively routed to the scanner context.
* **Triage Normalization:** Ensure the JSON payloads generated by these new rulesets map cleanly into our existing machine learning feature extraction dataframes without breaking matrix shape parameters.

## ML tier (if applicable)
- [x] Tier 1 — Triage (severity ranking, deduplication, false positive classification)
- [ ] Tier 2 — Predictive (fix success prediction, exploit scoring, pattern clustering)
- [ ] Tier 3 — Autonomous (LLM patch generation, self-healing pipeline)
- [ ] Not ML-related

## Alternatives considered
* **Integrating Checkov or Trivy binaries:** Rejected due to dependency bloat. Introducing multiple binaries complicates our environment definitions, requires independent engine configurations, and splits our JSON parsing architecture into fragile, multi-schema handlers.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Core scanner module successfully maps and triggers Semgrep IaC rules when relevant infrastructure files are present.
- [ ] File tree crawler correctly identifies and includes Dockerfiles, Terraform scripts, and workflow files without configuration loss.
- [ ] Expanded findings feed into the ML triage matrix cleanly without raising schema or feature alignment exceptions.
- [ ] Automated backend test suite includes updated assertions for the new infrastructure payloads while holding or exceeding our strict 35% coverage gate.

issues.title: [Bug]: Gitleaks security scan fails on pull_request_target event
issues.body:
## What happened
The newly implemented PR Guardrails CI workflow failed during the security scan phase. The `gitleaks-action` does not support the `pull_request_target` event trigger, causing a conflict when combined with the auto-labeling job that requires those elevated permissions.

## Steps to reproduce
1. Open a new pull request to trigger the combined PR Guardrails workflow.
2. The workflow attempts to run both labeling and Gitleaks under the `pull_request_target` event.
3. Check the Actions tab and observe the Gitleaks job failing immediately.

## Expected behaviour
Both the auto-labeling (template validation) and the Gitleaks security scan should execute and pass successfully without interfering with each other or throwing trigger-related errors.

## Actual behaviour
The labeling succeeds, but the Gitleaks scan fails and stops the pipeline with the following error: `Error: ERROR: The [pull_request_target] event is not yet supported`.

## Environment

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| OS | Ubuntu-latest (GitHub Actions Runner) |
| Python version | N/A |
| Node version | N/A |
| PatchPilot version / commit | main |
| Scan method | GitHub Actions Workflow |

## Logs
Warning: Unexpected input(s) 'report_format', valid inputs are ['']
► Run gitleaks/gitleaks-action@v2
Error: ERROR: The [pull_request_target] event is not yet supported

issues.title: [Feature] Enforce Code Quality Gates (Test Coverage & Stricter Linting)
issues.body:
## Summary
Enforce code quality gates via GitHub Actions to require minimum test coverage and strict Ruff linting on all new PRs.

## Motivation
As the project grows and more AI-generated code is introduced, we risk accumulating unused imports, messy formatting, and untested logic. Enforcing quality gates at the PR level ensures the codebase remains clean and unified, blocking lazy or poorly formatted PRs before a maintainer even has to look at them.

## Proposed solution
Implement a GitHub Actions workflow (e.g., `quality-gates.yml`) that acts as a strict gateway for incoming PRs:
1. **Test Coverage Enforcement:** Require new logic to have tests by running `pytest --cov` with a minimum threshold. 
2. **Linting Strictness:** Tighten `ruff` rules to automatically catch unused imports and enforce strict naming conventions/docstrings. The pipeline will fail if violations are found.

## ML tier (if applicable)
- [ ] Tier 1 — Triage (severity ranking, deduplication, false positive classification)
- [ ] Tier 2 — Predictive (fix success prediction, exploit scoring, pattern clustering)
- [ ] Tier 3 — Autonomous (LLM patch generation, self-healing pipeline)
- [x] Not ML-related

## Alternatives considered
Implementing Radon for complexity checks or Vulture for dead code detection. Ruled out for now because they often throw high false positives and require too much tweaking, which could frustrate beginner contributors.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The CI pipeline fails if a PR introduces new Python logic without corresponding tests (drops overall coverage).
- [ ] The CI pipeline fails if Ruff detects unused imports or styling violations.
- [ ] Baseline is established so existing passing code is not retroactively broken by the new strict rules.

## Additional context
Discussed in Discord. Keeping this isolated from the PR Guardrails issue to make reviewing and testing easier.

issues.title: [Feature] Implement Automated PR Guardrails (Labels, Security, Template Checks)
issues.body:
## Summary
Implement automated CI/CD guardrails to validate PR descriptions, auto-assign stack labels, and perform basic security scans.

## Motivation
Currently, maintainers have to manually check if contributors filled out the PR template, assign the correct labels, and hunt for basic security flaws. This takes time and is prone to human error. Automating this provides immediate feedback to contributors (especially beginners) and saves maintainer review time.

## Proposed solution
Implement a GitHub Actions workflow (e.g., `pr-guardrails.yml`) that triggers on PR open/edit. It will:
1. **Validate PR Descriptions:** Check if the standard PR template structure is intact. If missing, it automatically adds a `needs-work` label and comments asking for an update.
2. **Auto-Label:** Parse the PR description checkboxes and automatically assign the corresponding repository labels (`frontend`, `backend`, or `ml`).
3. **Security Scan:** Run basic SAST/Gitleaks on the incoming PR to block hardcoded secrets from entering the codebase.

## ML tier (if applicable)
- [ ] Tier 1 — Triage (severity ranking, deduplication, false positive classification)
- [ ] Tier 2 — Predictive (fix success prediction, exploit scoring, pattern clustering)
- [ ] Tier 3 — Autonomous (LLM patch generation, self-healing pipeline)
- [x] Not ML-related

## Alternatives considered
Using a third-party paid bot from the GitHub Marketplace was considered, but we want to keep dependencies low and use native GitHub Actions with open-source runners.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] PRs missing the standard template description get flagged with a `needs-work` label automatically.
- [ ] PRs with checked stack boxes get the corresponding labels applied without human intervention.
- [ ] PRs containing hardcoded secrets fail the CI check before review.

## Additional context
Discussed and approved by maintainers in Discord. I will take assignment and test these thoroughly on my own fork before opening the PR to avoid spamming the main repo with failed checks.

issues.title: refactor: improve B-Tree code structure and test coverage
issues.body:
Following the review on #178, the B-Tree implementation needs the following improvements.

**1. Generic sorted-array helpers**
Array shifting is manually repeated across leaf insertion, internal insertion, leaf deletion, borrow and merge operations. Extract into reusable static helpers:
- `insert_key_sorted()`
- `remove_key()`
- `shift_left()`
- `shift_right()`

**2. Generic child-pointer helpers**
Child array manipulation is duplicated across split, borrow and merge logic. Extract into:
- `insert_child()`
- `remove_child()`
- `shift_children_left()`
- `shift_children_right()`

**3. Common tree visualization framework**
B-Tree print/traversal could share infrastructure with BST/AVL printers.

**4. Shared multiway-tree utilities**
These are inlined inside insertion/deletion instead of being reusable helpers:
- `split_node()`
- `merge_nodes()`
- `borrow_from_left()`
- `borrow_from_right()`

**5. Generic validation/debug helpers missing**
- `validate_key_order()`
- `validate_leaf_links()`
- `validate_node_capacity()`
- `validate_tree_invariants()`

**6. Common search abstraction**
Search logic is implemented separately instead of following a common search interface used across tree structures.

**7. Existing B-Tree infrastructure**
Since B-Tree now exists, these concepts could potentially be shared between B-Tree and B+ Tree:
- node splitting
- borrowing
- merging
- traversal utilities

## Function-level refactoring

- Break `btree_delete` into smaller functions — it currently handles borrow left, borrow right, merge left, merge right and root adjustments all in one place
- Break `btree_insert_nonfull` split logic into dedicated helpers
- Add invariant checks after insert/delete during debugging

## Test coverage

- Stress tests: insert 10,000 keys, random deletes, random searches
- Memory allocation failure tests
- Add visualization of leaf linked-list structure

## Files affected

- `src/trees/btree.c`
- `tests/test_btree.c`

## Notes

No algorithm or logic changes. Purely refactoring and test coverage improvements. All existing tests must continue to pass.

issues.title: [Feature] Gamified Contributor Leaderboard (Score & PR Tracking)
issues.body:
## Summary
Implement a Contributor Leaderboard to track and gamify developer activity across the project. The leaderboard will track findings closed, fixes that pass verification, and merged PRs, calculating a weighted score for each contributor and displaying it in a premium UI.

## Motivation
Tracking contributor impact encourages engagement and provides visibility into who is driving security improvements and shipping verified fixes.

## Proposed solution

### Backend (Python/SQLite)
- Create a new `contributor_stats` table in SQLite with columns: `github_username`, `findings_closed`, `fixes_passed`, `prs_merged`, and `last_updated`.
- Add a `GET /leaderboard` endpoint that returns contributors sorted by a calculated weighted score:
  - Fix passed = 3 pts
  - PR merged = 2 pts
  - Finding closed = 1 pt
- Add a `POST /leaderboard/update` endpoint (manual trigger for V1) that parses a PR description for syntax like `Closes #XYZ` to map closed findings/fixes to the contributor.

### Frontend (React/Tailwind)
- Build a dedicated Leaderboard page/tab with a ranked table.
- Display columns for Rank, Avatar, Username, Findings Closed, Fixes Passed, and Total Score.
- **UI Polish:** 
  - Add visual progress bars behind the total score column to represent success rates.
  - Implement dynamic SVG/emoji badges (🥇, 🥈, 🥉) for the top 3 contributors.
  - Wrap contributor usernames in links pointing to `https://github.com/{username}`.

## ML tier (if applicable)
- [x] Not ML-related

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `contributor_stats` table migrations are applied successfully.
- [ ] Backend accurately calculates the weighted scores and returns sorted data.
- [ ] PR description regex successfully extracts issue numbers for attribution.
- [ ] Frontend displays the ranked table with avatars, badges, and accurate stats.
- [ ] Clicking a contributor's name correctly navigates to their GitHub profile.

issues.title: [Feature] Implement Supply Chain Diff to track dependency vulnerability changes
issues.body:
## Summary
Adds a visual Supply Chain Diff component comparing dependency vulnerabilities between the two most recent scans. This allows users to see exactly what security threats were introduced, resolved, or remained persistent after a dependency version bump.

## Motivation
When developers update packages in `package.json` or `requirements.txt`, they need immediate visibility into the security impact. A dedicated supply chain diff helps teams verify that a version bump actually resolved targeted vulnerabilities without introducing new supply chain risks.

## Proposed solution
1. **Backend:** Create a `GET /dependency-diff` endpoint in `main.py` that queries the SQLite database to fetch and compare findings from the latest two scan jobs, filtering strictly for the `dependency` category. It will return three distinct arrays: `introduced`, `resolved`, and `persistent`.
2. **Frontend Client:** Add `getDependencyDiff` to `api.ts` to handle the data fetch.
3. **UI Component:** Build a `dependency-diff.tsx` component using Tailwind CSS. It will reuse the split/stacked code diff design paradigm to render introduced threats with a red-tinted background badge/row and resolved threats with a green-tinted background and strikethrough styling.

## ML tier (if applicable)
- [x] Not ML-related

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Backend successfully diffs dependency findings between the two most recent scan jobs.
- [ ] The frontend displays introduced, resolved, and persistent vulnerabilities clearly in a responsive side-by-side or stacked card layout.
- [ ] Visual styling leverages Tailwind CSS color-coded indicators (red for introduced, green for resolved).
- [ ] Gracefully handles edge cases where only one scan exists or no changes are detected.

issues.title: [Enhancement] Track node position changes in undo/redo history stack
issues.body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
When dragging nodes in the Visual Graph builder, pressing Ctrl/Cmd+Z does not restore the node to its previous position. Currently the undo/redo stack only tracks:
- Node addition
- Node deletion
- Node duplication
- Config edits
- Edge creation/deletion

Node movement (drag) is not tracked.

**Describe the solution you'd like**
- Track node position changes in the undo/redo history stack
- Pressing Ctrl/Cmd+Z after moving a node should restore it to its previous position
- Pressing Ctrl/Cmd+Shift+Z should redo the movement
- Use ReactFlow's `onNodeDragStop` to capture snapshots — avoids flooding the history stack with intermediate drag positions

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
- Tracking every `onNodesChange` position update — rejected as it floods the history stack with too many intermediate states
- `onNodeDragStop` is cleaner as it only captures the final resting position after a drag

**Additional context**
- Follow-up to PR #72 which added keyboard shortcuts for workflow builder actions
- Requested by maintainer @vmDeshpande as a separate improvement
- Node positions are already stored inside `steps[].position`, so snapshots on `onNodeDragStop` should integrate cleanly with the existing `{ steps, edges }` history stack

issues.title: [Feature] Implement Side-by-Side Diff Preview for Code Fixes
issues.body:
## Summary
<!-- One sentence: what does this add or improve? -->
Implements a side-by-side visual diff preview for users to easily review AI-generated code fixes before applying them.

## Motivation
<!-- Why is this needed? What problem does it solve, or what value does it add? Be specific — "it would be nice" is less useful than "currently users have to X manually which causes Y". -->
Currently, reviewing code modifications without a structured visual diff makes it difficult to understand exactly what the AI changed. A clear side-by-side view allows users to quickly verify the safety and accuracy of a patch, removing the friction of manual off-platform verification and increasing the likelihood of users actually applying the fixes.

## Proposed solution
<!-- Describe your idea in detail. How should it work from the user's perspective? What would change in the UI, API, or output? -->
Introduce a dedicated React component (`DiffViewer`) that provides a two-column side-by-side layout. Under the hood, it will utilize the `diff` npm package to compute line-by-line differences between the original and modified code strings. A custom alignment function will process this diff data, injecting blank "spacer" blocks into the data arrays to ensure unchanged code remains perfectly synced vertically across both columns. Styling will be handled entirely via Tailwind CSS (using low-opacity green/red backgrounds for additions and removals) to mimic the native GitHub/VS Code review experience. The implementation will use pure JavaScript, strictly adhering to the project's non-TypeScript convention.

## ML tier (if applicable)
<!-- Is this related to the ML roadmap? Check one. -->
- [ ] Tier 1 — Triage (severity ranking, deduplication, false positive classification)
- [ ] Tier 2 — Predictive (fix success prediction, exploit scoring, pattern clustering)
- [ ] Tier 3 — Autonomous (LLM patch generation, self-healing pipeline)
- [x] Not ML-related

## Alternatives considered
<!-- Any other approaches you thought about, and why you ruled them out. Delete if not applicable. -->
- **Custom string comparison algorithm:** Ruled out due to the mathematical complexity and edge cases of string tokenization; using the established `diff` library is much safer, more reliable, and performant.

## Acceptance criteria
<!-- How would we know this feature is done? List 2–4 concrete, testable conditions. -->
- [ ] The UI successfully renders a two-column grid displaying the original code on the left and the proposed fix on the right.
- [ ] Added lines are highlighted with a green background on the right, and removed lines with a red background on the left.
- [ ] Both columns remain perfectly vertically aligned, dynamically utilizing blank spacer lines where code blocks have been added or removed.
- [ ] The UI relies exclusively on React and Tailwind CSS, introducing no new TypeScript configuration.

## Additional context
<!-- Links to papers, similar tools, screenshots, or anything else relevant. -->
The design goal is to closely mirror the industry-standard visual diffs seen in GitHub pull requests and VS Code to ensure an intuitive, zero-learning-curve developer experience.

issues.title: [Feature] /scan-url: add tests for URL validation and reachability checks

## Summary
<!-- One sentence: what does this add or improve? -->
Add unit tests for the `/scan-url` URL validation and reachability check logic added in #10.

## Motivation
<!-- Why is this needed? What problem does it solve, or what value does it add? Be specific — "it would be nice" is less useful than "currently users have to X manually which causes Y". -->

<!-- Describe your idea in detail. How should it work from the user's perspective? What would change in the UI, API, or output? -->

- Non-existent repo (404 from GitHub HEAD request) returns 422
- Network timeout returns 422 with "Could not reach GitHub" message
- Valid public repo passes reachability check and proceeds normally
